I have learned over the past two years since my last post that there is no
difference, really, between the business and academic fields when it comes
to the cut-throatness. It's all the same. Education, too, is becoming more and
more profit driven. I'll leave it at that for now. I'll, at some point, return to
share with you my life since September 2016. A lot of things had happened
and at one point I almost lost my life. I am sincerely happy to be alive.

So, as the title of this post suggests, I have been at an airport for the past 10
hours and counting. My employer who bought me the tickets, with or
without the knowledge of the 12-long-long layover hidden in these tickets,
confirmed them with the travel agent before speaking to me whether or not I
would be fine with it. Actually, I probably would not mind if I were told that

which would be the case. Caught off guard, I wasn't prepared anything.

I did not have the visa to leave Kiev Airport to see the city; hence, I could only
stay in the lounge trying to be as productive as I can. Now, what have I
learned during this 10 hours and counting? Let's begin with what I've been
doing for the last 10 hours. The first 3 hours were all devoted to catching on
sleep. From the fourth hour onward, I have been reading, writing, and
listening to a new audible books that I have just downloaded.

The book is Maps of Meaning: The Architecture of Belief by Professor Jordan
B. Peterson, whose works has become one of the authors that I've become
addicted to following. As a clinical psychologist who speaks with the clarity
and wit of a philosopher, he reminds me of great minds (and my heroes),
Professors Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tverksy (I'll also write about these
two at some point). In short, Professor Peterson wants us to think clearly
using logic and sciences.

He wants us to see the world the way it is -- no matter how discomfort it is
for it to be seen that way -- and not the way others try to convince you to
see it. Happiness, for instance, should not be the goal of life in Professor
Peterson's logic. Happiness should be something that you get out of doing
something moral and something right, for which he has created a set of
rational thoughts to help us think.

He blatantly criticizes identity politics driven by postmodern and hyper-left
activists to be detrimental to the improvement in which we are living. I, in
fact, finished his second book 12 Rules for Life: Antidote to Chaos first. It's a
great book which I highly recommend. It's worth it just to study his
storytelling technique -- it's a technique of a truly literary genius. It's this
book that got me thinking about the resonance between the 12 rules and my
12-hour-long layover.

So, I tried to write one rule that I hold dear in my life for every hour that has
passed. Again, I may return to rewrite these at some point too. I am sleepy
and fatigue as a result of not having been exposed to any sunlight at all for

the past ten hours trying to breath the limited amount of air available in a
badly ventilated airport terminal. Let's start with the first 6 rules for this
post: Social proof is dangerous.

We tend to not think too much when following what other people are already
or about to be doing. So, don't hang out with selfish people. You will be doing
awful thing before you even know it (and worse, sometimes you wouldn't
even know that which was bad); Be kind, not right.

It's simply the fact that we do not always know everything that we should
know about any particular situation to make an absolutely true judgement;
so, based on that, be kind to others and cut our own ignorance some big big
slack; Don't confuse what you want with what other people want you to
want. In our life, we have our own pace of moving forward.

Just because someone seems to be getting ahead of you doesn't mean that
you should always speed up, or worse, get off your own path to compete with
that person on his/her path. It's important to define your own path and walk
on it.

Freedom, alas, is the ability to set your own path and walk on it without
allowing any forms of arousal to distract you from it; Success needs both
determination (49%) and luck (51%) — by which I mean the kind of luck that
puts you in the right place at the right time.

But you first need the determination (AKA will power) — simply because
without it, you won't recognise luck when it appears (and you would just be
blaming other things or people for your failure); Do not tolerate a bad boss.
Bad boss is the parasite and deeply troubled symptom of evolution as a
whole. It's a disgrace to our humanities as a whole. If you continue to tolerate
and work under them, they will use their power to make you a scapegoat.

Histories have shown that which to always be the case. Do not tolerate bad
boss. Call them out. Period; and, Get older and wiser. Now, you are oldest
than you have even been. Don't become an old person who nobody wants to
see being alive. To be continued to Day 75!

                      12 Rules for Life

title: On Design Thinking Part 1: The Gist
excerpt: (PLEASE SKIP THIS PARAGRAPH TO the next if you are a first-time visitor to this blog. This first paragraph is going to be all about my confessions and apologies to the die-hard readers, especially those who're kind enough to subscribe to it…
body:
(PLEASE SKIP THIS PARAGRAPH TO the next if you are a first-time visitor to this

blog. This first paragraph is going to be all about my confessions and
apologies to the die-hard readers, especially those who're kind enough to
subscribe to it. So, yes, I have been rather lame. I thought I could get back to
writing again after my last post Day 75, after getting stuck at an airport's
terminal for 12 hours. Since then, I have been busy again and not been able to
get back to writing.

This post is something that I have been wanting to write for the past few
months. That said, the good news is that I now feel inspired again. Guess
what? Now I have a book to which I am looking forward; this blog is going to
become alive again!).

Over the past two years, I have been giving lectures, workshops, seminars
and talks on a goal- inspired multidisciplinary creative process known to
many as "design thinking." In this post, I want to summarize the three key
points of what I think design thinking entails, produces, and, most
importantly, is. This blog is the beginning of, hopefully, a long term and
consistent commitment on my part to keep up with this blog. Man, I have to,
at least, get to the 100th day as the name of the blog entails!

I will. That's not even a question, now that I have a much firmer ground of
what I want to write about over the next couple of months. It's one thing:
design thinking. It's the topic that I have been investing most of my waking
hours thinking, musing, and writing about. I have been approached by, and
for which I have been grateful to, a few top publishers in China who reveal to
me that there "isn't any good books on the topic of design thinking" in the
Chinese market.

I have always believed that China is the most important nation on earth in
the present -- and will continue to be so in years to come -- for the reason
that whatever it does will have a profound impact and effect on everyone on
this planet.

Hence, I feel as though my work (and eventually, at one point this year, a
book) will have a small contribution to how China may want to see its
development and growth vis-a-vis its responsibility -- ecologically,
economically, socioculturally -- as the most important nation on the planet.
Over the past two years, also, I have taught more than 60 courses to
hundreds of curious learners -- from primary school students to
entrepreneurs running companies of millions of dollars (and RMBs) industrial
assets.

I have learned so much along this continuing journey. In this first installment
of this design thinking threads, I want to talk about something closest to my
heart about design thinking: It's three most important principles. There are
many versions of and various popular meanings that some have given to the
term (in some 40 books and hundreds of blog posts available in the market at
the present).

The most important message I have been trying to convey, however, are the
following: Design thinking requires a much much "deeper" level of
participation (i.e., "Don't just ask what people think they want" principle --
most of us simply can not want what we do not know of; don't you agree?

); Design thinking requires us to imagine the world in which any status quo
does not existed (i.e., "How would you design something if we that something
has not existed before" principle); and, Design thinking does not always have
to require expensive invention (i.e., "Can't use a pen in space? Don't spend
money invent a pen to write in space, just use a pencil" principle). These are
the three most basic principles of design thinking. I will continue with the
details of these three points in my next post.

To be continued....

Gossiping, or, by Definition, a Crime
        Against Humanity

title: 12 Rules for Life
excerpt: A CONFESSION: I FORGOT TO continue writing this post! Many thanks to a big fan of this blog Bhawara for reminding me that I still owe her (and everyone of you) the last 6 rules. Well, but the fact that I also skipped Day 75 and wrote a new …
body:
A CONFESSION: I FORGOT TO continue writing this post! Many thanks to a big

fan of this blog Bhawara for reminding me that I still owe her (and everyone
of you) the last 6 rules. Well, but the fact that I also skipped Day 75 and wrote
a new post as Day 76 might be a sign of the fact that I knew that I was about
to skip Day 75.

Sigmund Freud would call this process of deliberate forgetting "parapraxis"
-- known to most of us as "Freudian slip" (thanks to the latest episode of
Malcolm Gladwell's Revisionist History podcast that gives me even deeper
insight to what it means). It could be the case that I wasn't ready to write this
post, which could be due to the very fact that I really did not have that many
rules in life.

I must confess, again, that the idea of "12 rules" wasn't entirely mine and was
that of Jordan B. Peterson whose bestseller 12 Rules For Life: An Antidote to
Chaos had inspired me to think very differently about me, my life, and my
own existence in the world. Dr Peterson is special to me in how he does not
simply give in to the nonsense -- and stick to his rules in living a meaningful
life. Think about it: What would life be without some, if not all,
meaningfulness?

Is there more to the reason for breathing, eating and seeking temporary
pleasure. I am not trying here to allude to religiosity (especially Buddhism
which is my main moral compass and guiding system of belief; in fact, it this
alluding were meant to direct somewhere, it would be more to the
philosophy of Stoicism rather than Buddhism). So, to make up for my
forgetfulness (please, please, please forgive me), I will reiterate and simplify
the first 6 rules before going into the next 6 rules. Like Dr.

Peterson's, these rules are simply "antidotes to chaos." I had failed at all of
these rules in the past and the results of such failure were wide ranging: from
hurting myself to hurting others, from a small thing that could be fixed to a
manmade disaster, and from a trivial human error to a PTSD-like kind of
trauma with which I will forever have to live. Human beings evolve because
we learn from mistakes of others, not by making those mistakes ourselves.

Can you imagine, if we should experience the biting of a snake before we can
learn that which isn't good for us? Well, we would all be dead already. So,
learn from my mistakes, please! I have not lived very long but I have seen,
experienced, read and studied quite a few things. I will, in this list, try to tell
these rules to you in a manner that combines both my limited personal
experience with what I have learned from, mainly, other people.

To make this list a bit more useful, I will also add the "practical advise" to the
end of each rule to make it easier for you to see how you may think about
adopting, or rejecting, some of these rules in your life. Stay away from selfish
people. There is a sea of scientific evidence from the field of social
psychological to support this rule. The gist of it is that what psychologists
call "social proof" is dangerous.

It's true that following others allows us to survive as a species. But that
doesn't mean that what a lot of people do is right. Don't do something just
because a lot of people do it. A practical advise for this rule: Don't hang out
with people who are either selfish or have bad faith. Such selfishness often
leads to justifications in engaging in many other things driven by bad faith.

You, too, will be doing awful thing before you even know it -- and worse,
sometimes you wouldn't even know that which was bad). In the spirit of
design thinking, there is even a saying that to be creative is to be able to
think of something as if that something has never been there before. In other
words, just because a lot of people use something doesn't mean that there's
no better option to that something.

It just means that such option has not yet to be created; Be kind, not right. I
myself had been in many situations that I would fight just to "be right" --
about a certain knowledge or moral principle -- just to, later on, lose a friend
(or friends), or worse, realize that I was fighting for what I wasn't even sure
of. It is the simply the fact that we do not always know everything that we
should know about any particular thing or situation.

To make an absolutely true judgement on anything, we're risking making a
bad decision. When Steven Jobs tells us to "stay hungry and stay foolish," he's
asking us to accept the fact that we can might as well be wrong on just about
everything we think we already know well based on whatever reasons we
have created for ourselves to believe so.

So, based on that, a practical advise is always be kind to others and cut our
own ignorance some big big slack; Live your own life, and not the life others
want you to live. In other words, don't confuse "what you want" with "what
other people want you to want." In our life, we have our own pace of moving
forward.

Just because someone or some people seems to be getting ahead of you
doesn't mean that you should always speed up, or worse, get off your own
path to compete with that person on his/her path. A practical advise: It's
important to define your own path based on what you want to be and what
makes you feel content -- and, most importantly, stick to it.

I shall cite one of my favorite thinkers (although I do not always agree with
his ideas) Immanuel Kant who writes (and I paraphrase because I am not that
good at memorizing the exact quote) that "Freedom, alas, is the ability to set

your own path and walk on it without allowing any forms of arousal to
distract you from it"; Don't compete senselessly, but always be ready for
challenges.

My mentor MIT Professor Jan Wampler once said to me that, for him, his
success is a result of 99% of hardwork on his part, leaving just 1% for his
intelligence. I want to revise this formula, success needs both determination
(49%) and luck (51%) — by which I mean the kind of luck that puts you in the
right place at the right time.

But you first need the determination (AKA will power) — simply because
without it, you won't recognize luck when it appears (and you would just be
blaming other things or people for your failure). So, a practical advise is:
Follow your own path at your own pace, but don't be too complacent and
always stay alert and curious for the opportunities, which you will only see
them only if you are trained to; Do not tolerate a bad boss.

My father once said that a bad boss is the parasite and deeply troubled
symptom of evolution as a whole. It's a disgrace to our humanities as a whole.
They are the reason for our transgression. This rule goes hand-in-hand with
the first rule, but with a larger impact. If work is what makes us content with
life and gives meaning to what we do, you want your work to define who you
are -- and you can only do that under a boss whom you respect.

If you continue to tolerate and work under them, they will use their power to
make you a scapegoat. Histories have shown that which to always be the
case. A practical advise is the same as the rule itself: Do not tolerate bad boss.
Call them out. Cut them off, and move on. Period; Get older and wiser. The
philosopher Cicero once remarked about the beauty of getting old, and we
should all embrace the very same philosophy. Getting old naturally is great!

We become less a slave to our biological desire as we grow old -- please love
that feeling! A practical advise is to try to be that old person that younger
folks want to come and learn from your embedded and built wisdom, Don't
become an old person who nobody wants to see being alive. Now, you are

oldest than you have even been; Do not give a fuck about everything (but give
a fuck about something that matters to you).

In The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck by the today's most famous successful
writer in the world Mark Manson, the author argues that we only have a
limited amount of fuck to give in life, so if we give a fuck about everything
that comes our way we won't have any of it left to give to what really matters.
I first thought that he was apt to say something so true in such a witty way.

Then, I soon realized that what Manson means is based on something much
more scientific: According to the principles of cognitive psychology, we have
very limited cognitive capacity, so we can't let ourselves be dwelled by all
socially-induced thoughts that come our way. You may have heard of how
science has shown that we can only keep up with a certain number of people
in life, we can only engage in so many multi-tasking responsibilities, and so
on.

The same goes with this very key idea about not giving a fuck about what
don't matter.

The truth is that we can be almost certain that most of the time we will fail to
control what people think about you (what people think about you is in their
heads -- not yours!); so the practical advise is to accept such fact that only
try your best to engage in what you can control -- your own mind to not
giving a fuck too much to too many things of which you are not in a position
to be in control; You do not need to have an opinion on everything.

It's true that we tend to want to have an opinion on everything. Why is the
desk you're working on your laptop right now not as clean as you want it to
be? Why is the cup you're using not as nice as the one you used to hold? Why
is the spoon you are using looks so strange?

Why is it that the cover of the book the person sitting across the row from
your in orange and not in white (it would be much better if it were to be in
white)? Why is the world not going the way you want? You get the point.
There're so many things in life that you either 1) Do not have enough

knowledge about to have an opinion?

-- what do you know about the ergonomic design; engineering and building
construction; mining, molding, and melting process; and publishing industry
and its complicated advertising campaign to know about the design of the
desk, cup, spoon and book cover? Even if you happen to be a keen industrial
designer who knows everything about a desk, cup, spoon and book cover,
you must still be practical and flexible.

I like to live by the wisdom of the economist John Maynard Keynes: "When
information changes, change your opinion." Again, not only do we not know
everything in the world, but for many things that we think we know there can
also be new data, research, information to disprove it; and when that
happens those who can adjust will benefit. And, 2) There are so many things
in life that do not matter to you at all whether you do or do not have any
opinions about them.

Restrain yourself from wanting to show off how much you want other people
to think you know by criticizing everything. A practical advise is to look at
things the way they are, and appreciate them that way -- if you can; Don't be
a victim to your own ability to think about something.

It's a blessing to have a brain to be able to think, but it's also a curse that we
tend to focus too much on what we think about while we're thinking about it
-- to the point that we often, if not always, miss the point. Confusing? That's
intentional by the way. Let me try again.

My favorite thinker Professor Daniel Kahneman once said "Nothing in life is
as important as you think it is, while you are thinking about it." What Dani
means here is that we tend to fall into the trap of what he calls "focusing
illusion." We tend to give too much weight to what we're thinking about --
especially while you are thinking about it. So, a practical advise is whenever
you are writing an important email, don't send it after at least 24 hours.

It's backed by science that you are likely no longer wanting to send it after
having read it again while you are not "obsessively thinking about it"; Be

distrustful of gossips. People do gossip -- and many argue that it's our
nature. That's actually not quite right according to what we know about the
science of human's evolution. What's our nature, argued by the literary
theorist Jonathan Gottschall, is not gossiping, but telling stories. We love
stories.

Gottschall calls humans "storytelling animals." We also tend to like to create
stories that are based on something that we are NOT certain about. The
anthropologist Michael Herzfeld always tells his audiences that anthropology
is the study of gossips, and he is spot on. Myths, ultimately, are forms of
gossips speculating how the world was pieced together and how might we --
small people who weren't there when it happened -- deal with our own
existence. It's this very ingredient that makes stories interesting,
suspenseful, gripping and lovable by readers.

Gossips, in many ways, are "highly entertaining lies" put together with some
grains of reality and that's why we love to justify hearing them. Why are
gossips so entertaining? First, it's not about you, so it's like watching an
intensely drama in the comfort of your own armchair. Second, it promises
"exclusiveness," making you feel important to be chosen as the one to whom
the intimate story is to be told.

Third, it speaks to what you feel lacking in your life -- in other words, gossips
"fill some of the voids of your malignant desire," allowing you to tell yourself
that the unsatisfied situation in which you are currently is because of
someone else and not because of you. Gossips have so much power to shape
our perception on or against someone or something. And many times
because we believe gossips, we misjudge people, situation, and things. That's
unfair to humanities. In fact, Pope Francis himself said something extremely
similar.

He, in fact, compares gossips to "bombs," and people who gossip to
"terrorists." What the Pope said encompasses all the three aforementioned
points of why gossips are, in this sense, sinful: "Because gossip is like a bomb.
One throws it, it causes destruction and you walk away tranquilly." Some

even go as far as to say that, even though stories are fun to learn, we are
most peaceful and content with life when we don't make up stories in our
head.

Buddhism monks choose to chant lines from ancient scriptures that they are
not fully understand so that they can stay away from making up stories.
That's the only way to be calm. I personally stop people from telling me
gossips. A practical advise here is simple: Resist the temptation to make up a
story that you do not have any solid "court case" kind of evidence to back it
up; and, even easier, stay away from listening to any of it.

There's a lot of scientific evidence in Steven Pinker's new book that the
quality of your life once you have disengage yourself from the availability of
an unevenly distributed but highly entertaining fabricated information.

Or if you don't trust Professor Pinker, you could perhaps trust the Pope when
he said "No gossip -- and know that the best remedy against gossip is to bite
your tongue" What's the point of being a terrorist who carries a bomb with
you wherever you go?; Don't attach yourself to an image of anything -- even
if that thing used to be what you most revere and respect. In my very own
religion Buddhism, any forms of attachment is a source of suffering. Like
people, things change.

When time changes, things also change with it. I used to get so upset over
the fact that someone I once loved no longer loved me, to the point that I was
almost driven to a complete self-destruction. It's until I realized that I was
too attached -- not to this lover -- but the image of her that I wanted to stay
permanently, disregarding the fact that she must grow, change, and become
whoever she wanted to be in life.

So, this kind of attachment not only disrespects the nature of our universe
(AKA the fact that time does exist) but also disregard the individual's right to
change his/her self image. This also applies to your own self image. How do
you think you could freeze that despite your becoming older daily as well as
the circumstances surrounding such change? Come on, dude: don't be a jerk

and be respectful. A practical advise?

Do thank the owner of the restaurant you have been going to for years for
keeping the restaurant open even if the food is no longer as good as it was. If
the owner asks you why you thank him/her, tell him/her that you have
detached yourself from the curiously delicious image of the food that the
restaurant once served. LOL and kidding. Just say thank you again; and,
finally the last rule, Don't try to change the world -- try to change yourself.

A while ago I wrote a personal memoir in a form of a meme to myself that "I
do not want to compete with anyone, and all I want is to be better than
yesterday." In design thinking, we like to think that we can "prototype" our
life simply by eliminating what we no longer want in our life (e.g., smoking,
drinking) when we get up in the morning and trying the live that life for a
week.

If it works out just fine, the prototype works and we can go on living our life
based on it. If it doesn't, then we go back to see what can be fixed, tweaked
or re-booted in order to achieve the goal. Dr Peterson provides something
quite similar in one of his 12 rules: "Rule 6: Set your house in perfect order
before you criticize the world." Actually, I would go as far as to never try to
criticize the world.

The world will change when you change the way you view it -- with a clearer
mind of a constantly prototyping life. Up to this point, now I know why Dr.
Peterson needs to write a book to discuss his 12 rules -- I couldn't easily do
the same thing in fact. There're so much to talk about concerning the reason
for each of these rules to exist. A practical advise: Life is only meaningful
when you see, think, and experience it as fun.

So, try to get up in the morning knowing what you want to do on that very
day and, don't be too greedy, try to live one day at a time.

On Design Thinking Part 1: The Gist

title: Gossiping, or, by Definition, a Crime Against Humanity
excerpt: I WAS INITIALLY PLANNING TO continue the writing of my newest series on design thinking, of which the first part had been written as the Day 76 post. I, however, have been engaging in many meaningful discussions with friends, colleagues, an…
body:
I WAS INITIALLY PLANNING TO continue the writing of my newest series on

design thinking, of which the first part had been written as the Day 76 post. I,
however, have been engaging in many meaningful discussions with friends,
colleagues, and guests of this blog who were highly entertained by my last
post on the 12 rules for life, especially on the tenth rule: Be distrustful of
gossip.

In fact, I think the name of this tenth rule should be changed to the very title
of this post: Gossip is a crime against humanity. Coincidentally, the feeling
that this title radiates coincides with Pope Francis' who has recently warned
us against both conscious and unconscious engagement in the "terrorism" of
gossip. In other words, gossip is murder. I, too, wholeheartedly disdain
gossip. It all began when I was young.

My father was a kind man; so it's no surprise that I barely remember a
situation in which I was being yelled at by him. Yet, I remember the one and
probably the only instance very well. It was because I told him what I thought
I knew about my classmate -- gossip about his cheating at an exam. My
father immediately asked me, " How did you know for sure that he cheated?

Did you see it yourself?

Did you report it?" My response was clumsy: I told my father that I "didn't
actually know." And, in fact, I heard about this cheating from another
classmate who was probably told about this cheating by another classmate.
That was it for my kindest father: He didn't snap (I would have if I were him,
knowing what kind of man he always was) but he said to me: "There's no
room for gossip in this house." He went on to talk to me about the
destructive power of gossip.

In fact, I remember well how he said: It's your responsibility to report this
cheating if you have the evidence that it happens. We do not accuse people
without any evidence. On the other hand, if we have the evidence of a crime
and don't act, we are no different from being a criminal ourselves. That's why
I asked you How did y ou know for sure that he cheated? Did you see it
yourself? Did you report it?

So, like my father, I believe that gossip is a sign of cowardice, immaturity,
insecurity, weakness and lack of moral capacity to act on one's own. It's also
a sign of envy and unconstructive self-doubt. I'll write more about these
unwanted qualities in a person in the later part of this post. If I were to
continue with the pontiff's metaphor of terrorism, gossiping is nothing less
than throwing a bomb at someone at than walk away with no responsibility
(the point which I will get back to later).

Writing this blog post also as a trained anthropologist, however, I also
recognize how gossip shed light on the micro politics of the particular
community -- which, I would then argue, should not in any way justify the
existence of gossip. The anthropologist Michael Herzfeld often says how he
sees anthropology as a study of gossips.

He derives this very understanding both from the Greek etymology of the
term ( anthrpos and logia ) and the social theory of his predecessor the
British anthropologist Max Gluckman who partly argues for the legitimacy of
the study of gossip and scandal in modern anthropology.

Herzfeld argues that what makes rich ethnography is "when anthropologists
are trying to get their informants to get off the stage and whisper all those
intimate and slightly disreputable bits of gossip that make good, juicy
ethnographic data." That said, there is something quite unsettling about such
statement. This is because of my having learned over the years that my
informants tend to walk out of a gossipy conversation as much more
stressful, angry and aggressive individuals after having gossiping.

Like Gluckman and Herzfeld, I used to believe in gossip as social duty and
one should not be stressful, angry and aggressive when engaging in such
duty. That said, over the years I have learned that which is not always the
case. When two parties were in conflict with one another, despite the power
dynamics, things often get solved more effectively when those two parties
engage in a direct and transparent and form of nonviolent communication
(which I occasionally had the pleasure of moderating).

To me, hearing their gossip feels like my throwing an emotional bomb at
them. I would go so far as to say that it's unethical to motivate the informants
to engage in gossiping. Why do I insist on a gossip-free ethnography despite
the misopportunity to get "juicy ethnographic data? Should we, in the name
of knowledge, do everything to get data?

Just because gossip tells anthropologists about the tension between
members of a particular community -- and therefore makes "juicy
ethnographic data" - - it doesn't mean that these anthropologists have the
solid moral ground on which they could build their case in pursuit of
knowledge to encourage their informants to engage in the act of terrorism
against one another, especially just to "get more juicy ethnographic data." An
anthropology colleague summarizes it vividly: Gossip often harbors malicious
intent.

Frustration at others can often boil over into anger and malice leading to
gossip. Anthropologist should understand frustration, anger and malice, but
not encourage it. That is, it would be like getting someone to throw a bomb
at each other so that you can, in the name of the benign anatomical science,

study how their bodies disintegrate. There're many other ways that
anthropologists can learn about a certain society. In my own anthropological
research, I have never sat through a session of gossip.

Well, there might be a few instances or two that my language skill wasn't
good enough for me to know whether I was about to be listening to gossip.
But when I am fully conscious and in control of the command of the language
used in the conversation, I always make sure to my informants that I am only
interested in hearing from them their perceived experiences, and not their
personal attacks on other people.

Sometimes I let my informants share with me their feeling of discontent
(that's why friends are for, isn't it), but it's known among my circle of
informants that I would interject with a question "How do you know that?"
should the sharing begin to incorporate any forms of gossip. This strategy
occasionally urges my informants to re-evaluate their assumption and
prejudice.

It's also good for my research to make sure that my informants know that I
am not the kind who is fond of gossip -- as those who is fond of gossip tend
to judge, take side, and engage in malicious action of coward betrayal. What
would your position be in a community that you study if all of your
informants think of you as a gossip's collector? In other words, in the
language     of   nonviolent   communication,   I   am    only   interested     in
"evaluation-free" prose.

Many might argue that this is not the right way to do ethnography, but I
somehow insist on it. I sincerely do not want my view on anyone in the
community where I study to be clouded by the personal evaluation of the
informants who might like or hate one another. Of course, once the close
friendliness with the informants is established, my informants and I are
prone to share with each other something deeper information- wise.

They would ask me about my personal life, e.g., my love life and whether or
not I am married. My answer would also be evaluation-free, such as, "No, I

don't have a girlfriend." And if they insist on knowing whether there's anyone,
I would tell them right away. There's nothing to hide among friends, I believe.
That said, I always tell my informants that it's more important for me to learn
about what actually has happened or is happening, rather than what they
think who did what.

In my research, I have other methodological means, such as participant
observation, to understand those without having to resort to gossip. So, it
may be true that my ethnographic data is not as "juicy" as it could be. I can
live with it. By writing this blog post, I hope to convince you, with all my
knowledge, why gossip should have no place in the society in which we are
living in today, and why all of our effort would help to make that happen.

I will prove to you that gossip are like bullets from an automatic gun; once
you let it be heard by your ears, you will be wounded the same way you let a
bullet hit your body. Your judgement will be clouded with biases, which will
lead to many decisions that you otherwise would be in a better position to
make. P.S.

I was blessed by these amazing readers with a series of thoughtful
discussions, especially with the philosopher Dr Mark Calano and a recent
graduate and my former student and now a good friend Robert Zajc who,
probably, is one of the most curious and remarkable students I have ever had
the pleasure of teaching. My mother too contributed quite a few great
thoughts during our ride to a shopping mall this morning. So, thank you,
Mark, Robert and my dearest mother for inspiring this blog post.

How did it all begin? Humans don't always gossip -- what we always do is
telling stories. In a griping prose, the writer Jonathan Gottschall tells us in his
bestseller The Storytelling Animal: How Stories Make Us Human about how
we have always been prone to create, make up, and piece together stories.
"We are wired for stories," said he. Archeological evidence of ancient
paintings and scriptures on the wall are evidence of that.

As Gottschall's research shows, we humans make sense of the world by
creating stories that are based on something that we are not certain about.
Religious myths and stories about the origins of the world, ultimately, are
forms of gossip, speculating how the world was put together -- because none
of us were there when that happened. Gossips, in many ways, are "unproven
speculations" put together based on some grains of possibility observable in
real life. That might explain why we love to justify hearing them.

Anthropologists and historians argued that some sort of structure of power
emerged and the sense of repression rose at some point in the short history
of our existence (compared to that of the universe). Long story short: Those
who saw themselves as being in the weaker position began to communicate,
among themselves, their anxiety, fear, and discontent toward those whom
they saw as being in a superior position. In his best work Weapons of the
Weak Everyday Forms of Peasant Resistance, the political scientists James C.

Scott coins the term "weapons of the weak" to describe gossip as one of
many weapons used in the manner of "hidden transcripts" by the weaker
population against the superior power to whom the the weaker population
have no "official" source of gauging and voicing their discontent with each
other. Scott argues that "gossip as hidden transcripts" are important because
otherwise the individual who sees himself/herself as weak would no clue
whether he/she is the only one feeling that way.

So, by gossiping, these individuals get to gauge the landscape of discontent.
Most of us must have had this feeling: How many times that we talk to voice
our sense of worry to our friends and colleagues when we feel as though we
should have been treated better by our boss, teacher, superior -- even
though we know that the best way to actually get to know whether that
which is the case is to go and speak directly to the boss, teacher, superior?

The argument that Herzfeld's predecessor the anthropologist Max Gluckman
has made in his monumental paper "Gossip and Scandal" published in 1963 is
similar to mine: Because unlike an open discussion, the closed-circuit nature
of gossip create the kind of "exclusiveness" that plays a role both in

indoctrinating a new member to the social group, unite the existing social
group, as well as preventing any strangers from the group.

In this sense, it's the weak who needs the function of gossip to stabilize the
existence of social group to give them some sense of security. Gossip, on the
other hand, also have the function of maintaining the morals, unity, and
values of social groups for which those who consider themselves to be more
powerful also have an important role to play.

The argument here, however, lies in the idea that those in the powerful
position know that the weaker population (which can be those with the
younger, new, or those with pre-designated lower tier membership) are
aware of their inferior status and is actually more susceptible to "informal
activities" that define the unity and membership of the group. These
information activities are obviously not written in their job descriptions but
are essentially to their not being a victim of vicious gossip.

In any companies, for example, we all know that it is not enough just to be a
good employee. In many countries, many employees attend social activities
(such as drinking) with their bosses even though they don't have the financial
means or time to conveniently do so. Why do they still have to do it?

Because they know that even though their bosses cannot use their absence
from these social activities to fire them, they can easily be prejudiced against
because of their lack of participation in informal activities that is understood
by the norm as uniting and defining the membership of the group. This
situation also works sideway among those who are in the same social status
but fear of losing such status.

In other words, why do many of us spend money we don't really have to buy
things we don't even want jus to please those whom we don't even like?
Gossip, in this sense, is intentionally created either to: Undermine the
authority to which the weak do not have the "formal" power to fight against;
and/or, Undermine the power of the weak by the authority who know that
the weak tend to believe that they can undermine the authority by using

gossip.

In other words, those who see themselves in an inferior position are also
made to believe that they "actually" have the power to undermine the
authority while what they are actually doing is to play by the authority's
game. The social psychologist Robert B. Cialdini shows in his recent research
about the power of publicity that those in the position of power and/or
authority actually like those who see themselves in the lower position to
gossip about them.

Summarizing in a short quote "There's no such thing as a bad publicity," this
principle of, what Cialdini calls, "pre-suation" plays a role in constantly
injecting the image of the authority in the mind of those who think that they
could confront them through a back door. In Pre- Suasion: A Revolutionary
Way to Influence and Persuade, Cialdini summarizes: It's often said that the
fate celebrities fear most is to be ignored, forgotten, or otherwise dropped
from the cultural consciousness.

Powerful publicity of any sort spares them that worst of all fates because it
brings them attention; and raw attention anoints them with presumed
importance. " The last two words "presumed importance" is key, and now
that we know this we must not forget that we can be complicit in promoting
the power structure simply by gossiping about them. The line between
gossiping to gauge the collective discontent and gossiping to make those
powerful even more presence in terms of presumed importance is blurry.

In fact, even the anthropologist Max Gluckman himself doesn't think just
because gossips are "useful" to the study of social anthropology we should
simply endorse it. He actually says quite the opposite alluding to the danger
of doing so: My argument about gossip and scandal is similar: If I suggest that
gossip and scandal are socially virtuous and valuable, this does not mean that
I always approve of them.

Indeed, in practice I find that when I am gossiping about my friends as well as
my enemies I am deeply conscious of performing a social duty; but that when

I hear they gossip viciously about me, I am rightfully filled with righteous
indignation. For those who justify gossiping as an heroic act, it may be
dismaying for them to hear that gossip don't lead to a successful revolution.
Gossip may help those who see themselves in an inferior position to know
that they are not alone in the battle.

But, ultimately, it's the selfless act of those who are willing to risk everything
confronting the injustice who gathers the momentum of public support to
win the fight. Gossip is often seen a sign of immaturity, insecurity and
parasitic aspect of those who are prone to emotional violence. Those who
gossip, therefore, do not have the social credit let alone the charm or
charisma to lead the revolution. Staging a revolution against the oppress
requires much more than constructed narratives. The difference between
useful information and gossip is to follow.

Why are gossip so entertaining? As I wrote in my last post (and quite rightly
and     accidentally   coinciding   with     Gluckman's   argument)   gossip    are
entertaining because they are -- and here I am also alluding to my concern of
this kind of vicious entertainment: Not about you -- so, listening to gossip is
like watching a bomb being thrown at and killed civilians at the comfort of
your armchair; and telling gossip is like throwing a bomb at an innocent
people and walk away without any responsibilities.

As Dr Dennis Hunnell MD writes, "Gossip has little regard for the human
dignity of its victim"; Gives you the sense of importance through
exclusiveness -- when telling or listening to gossip, you are creating an
exclusive club consisting of invited-only guests to join an orgy of terrorists;
and, Fulfill the desire for vengeance -- gossip makes you feel as though
someone else is responsible for the situation in which you are. Why are
gossip so bad?

Gossip create an image of a thing, person, and event before you yourself
experience it. This image is what social psychologists believe to lead to a kind
of systematic prejudice commonly known as "confirmation bias," which is a
kind of mental shortcut that we use to make most of the decisions in our life.

Say, you have heard a gossip about your newest office's colleague "being
lazy." And say you don't have any other information about this person. On
Monday's morning, you're then asked to mentor this new colleague.

It's very likely that you would take the gossip that you have heard about his
laziness as a heuristics of "mental shortcut" in dealing with him during his
first assignment with you. You will be likely to watch him carefully and try to
find any sign that confirms your bias about his laziness, and it's very likely
that you will find that sign -- not just one and many. Why? Because you are
deliberately and carefully looking for it. Now imagine if the situation is a
reverse.

You're this newest colleague. You did not do anything wrong and you were
not lazy -- but everything you did was considered laziness just because that
person who was supposed to be mentoring you saw you through his
"confirmation bias lens" induced by gossip. Would you be okay with that?
This goes almost with everything you experience in your life.

The comedian John Oliver once showed how confirmation bias work in a
episode of his award-winning Last Week Tonight show, during which he
presented a few yet-to-be-fully-investigated scandals about Hilary Clinton.
And then he went on to mention a few other scandals of hers, such as the
Swiss File Transfer case to which most people simply knotted their heads.
And then Oliver surprisingly revealed that "I just made up the Swiss File
Transfer case...but for a second, you kind of remembered it." Let's put that to
a test.

Read the following passage. You have a need for other people to like and
admire you, and yet you tend to be critical of yourself. While you have some
personality weaknesses, you are generally able to compensate for them. You
have considerable unused capacity that you have not turned to your
advantage. Disciplined and self-controlled on the outside, you tend to be
worried and insecure on the inside. At times you have serious doubts as to
whether you have made the right decision or done the right thing.

You prefer a certain amount of change and variety and become dissatisfied
when hemmed in by restrictions and limitations. You also pride yourself on
being an independent thinker and do not accept others' statements without
satisfactory proof. But you have found it unwise to be too frank in revealing
yourself to others. At times you are extroverted, affable, and sociable, while
at other times you are introverted, wary, and reserved. Some of your
aspirations tend to be rather unrealistic. Done? Now, go back and read it
again.

This time do keep in mind that this is a statement describing YOU based on
hundreds of thousands of data points that I've collected from the comments,
e-mails, and other browsing information generated by WordPress.com all
cross-referenced with demographics information over the half-life of this
very blog. The process of doing so has been long and tedious, but I did so any
way to come up with this statement about you. So, trust me on this, I have a
good idea of who you are.

Now, please go back and read it again. Now what do you think? Does it sound
like you, my dearest reader? It should, but not because of how this
paragraphs is written based on any data that I just described, but because it's
written in such a broad and vague term that it should describe everyone.

The tendency that you are likely to think that this is about you the second
time you read it is because of your unconscious using of the "mental
shortcut" to make sense of this paragraph vis-a-vis you. Everything seems to
fall into its place, isn't it? You may be disappointed to learn that the
paragraph above comes from David McRaney's book You Are Not So Smart,
which describes, tests, and provide scientific findings of this kind of mental
shortcuts.

The paragraph above, in fact, come from a 1948 experiment by the Stanford
University psychologist Bertram R. Forer in his study of, what he later terms,
"subjective validation" showing that we are all prone to believing vague
statements and predictions, especially if they are positive and address you
personally -- in this case, through the use of, also, our proneness to be

prejudiced, anchored, and mislead by a tactic of confirmation bias (i.e., the
entire paragraph in red above).

In the example above about your newest colleague at your old workplace,
you might just want to protect yourself from being taken advantage of by this
"known to be (through gossip as being) lazy" newest colleague; therefore, you
justify using gossip as a base of the information on how to avoid being taken
advantage of. That said, why would you be afraid of your being taken
advantage of if you're certain of your ability in your role to mentor a new
person joining the office.

Even if he's lazy, isn't that one of the behavioral and cognitive change that
you can help this person change through your role as his mentor? Because
gossip can also easily grow up of jealously and senseless egotism, people who
constantly engage in gossiping entertained themselves at the expense of
their own ability to grow out of their own self-imposed immaturity and
cultivate their sense of self efficacy.

At a personal level, to emphasize, gossip only represents your immaturity,
insecurity, and your desire to be parasitic to the community of which you
have become a member. In other words, those who gossip tend to have
sociopathic personality -- in the sense that they lack conscience and do not
care about the negative consequences of their action on other people. At a
societal level, gossip create an image of a society that is worse than before.

The psychologist Steven Pinker has written in many occasions about how
many of us think of the world as being more violent when, in reality, we are
actually living in the most peaceful time in the history. As Pinker writes, a
news about a plane safely landed at an airport doesn't make a headline
whereas a news about a slight disputation of an engine of a plane causing an
emergency landing; so, we tend to hear more about bad news than good.

Owing to that nature of media, we tend to think of the world as becoming
more dangerous when all statistics available are pointing to the opposite. The
great psychologists Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman in 1974 calls this

kind of cognitive fallacy "availability bias," which clouds us from seeing the
situation, circumstance, and the world the way it is.

The same goes with how we tend to hear news about someone's winning a
jackpot and not millions of people who wasted their precious bucks on a
piece of useless paper they once call lottery. This might explain why those
who watch news tend to buy both lottery tickets and travel insurance when
flying? Gossip are like that too.

We like to hear stories that fill the void of emptiness in our soul and that
usually means we like to hear a drama involving how others are not as
superior as we are. Gossip, in this sense, only makes us see the world
through the same lens as those ubiquitous bad news we see in the media.
Sooner or later we will soon think that most planes crash, flying is not safe,
and most people in the world want to kill you (and therefore you must act
first).

Rules Against Gossip and for the Humanity There's only one rule: Don't
engage. What it means is that you: Should not listen to any of it -- again, I
must emphasize that gossip are (not just like, they are) bombs and bullets to
the soul and to your character. So, literally walk away from it when someone
is about to say it as if you are walking away from someone who is about to
throw a bomb and shoot you in the chest; and, Don't gossip.

It's obvious that the second one is much harder than the first. For the first
rule, I usually just walk away. In a situation in which walking away is not
possible, I change the topic or politely make it clear to the counterpart that I
do not wish to engage. There is no possible justification for letting yourself
hit by the bullets of a terrorist who is selfish and is only see you as an object
of malignant desire.

Again, you could use my father's words "Give me a hard evidence -- There's
no room for gossip in this room/table/meeting." It is hard to avoid engaging
in gossip if we do not first distinguish it from, simply, "informational prose." A
few friends asked me whether it's okay for them to tell what they have heard

-- what they call "a piece of truthful information." First, you need to know
difference between an information and a gossip? Try to point out the
difference between these two sentences.

A hint here is that one is considered to be a piece of information that you
happen to know, and the other is a gossip. The situation/context here is that
you're being asked whether you know that Mr. Adams is married. "Mr. Adams
told me that he is married." "Mr. Adams said he's married, although I
personally do not believe him." I hope that you can tell right away that the
first sentence is an informational while the second is not. If Mr.

Adams has given you just one piece of information that he's married -- in this
case, he must have "told" you in person, which is different from your looking
at his personal profile and saw the box "married" checked without his
consent. You do not need to add any of your personal thought to the truth
that Mr. Adams told you that he's married. The added information about your
not believing his words is considered unnecessary and gossipy.

This is the kind of information that will consciously or unconsciously cloud
the judgment of the listener. For whatever moral justification you have
regarding your assertion of your personal belief, the consequence of this
unnecessary prejudice you have created is on you. Just to be clear, let's have
another exercise. Which of these is gossip? I did not see Mr. Boyles in my
class today. Mr. Boyles deliberately didn't come to class because he doesn't
like the teacher. In other words, Dr.

Marshall Rosenberg says, "do not confuse observation with evaluation" (i.e.,
the #1 principle). He writes in his monumental theory of non-violent
communication: Differentiating observation from evaluation, being able to
carefully observe what is happening free of evaluation, and to specify
behaviors and conditions that are affecting us is the first skill of non-violent
communication. When you are telling what you are observing, you are in a
much better place to not be prone to gossip. Another way to think about it is
to abide by Dr.

Peterson's Rule#8, which is "Tell the truth – or, at least, don't lie." You might
argue that you are not lying when you add "although I personally do not
believe him." Well, don't try to be too clever here. Yes, you won't be lying
when you share your honest opinion if you really feel so, but based on what?
Your personal opinion?

How do you know that your personal opinion is not based on any other forms
of prejudice that you probably have heard from other sources -- such as
gossips -- that someone has shared with you. Just because you don't know
whether what make you think you know is not a lie does not give you a moral
justification to convey it as a truth, even with your most honest opinion.

In other words, just because you "think" you are telling your honest opinion
doesn't mean that you are not lying. In fact, in his 1 2 Rules for Life: An
Antidote to Chaos book, he outlines very thoughtfully what lies, and forms of
lies based on your own belief that what you think you know is not a lie, can
hurt you pathologically and emotionally.

So, in this case, saying what you know for certain and, to your most honest
mind, that which is not a lie -- the very fact that Mr. Adams told you that he
is married -- is not just enough but appropriate. Any information added to
this is considered gossipy. Don't tell gossips -- Bite your tongue if you need
to! We love stories; the more is the better, and the more of them is the more
convincing the argument is.

I actually understand how hard it is to stop at "Mr. Adams told me that he is
married" or simply "I did not see Mr. Boyles in my class today." In the study
of, what later on called "conjunction fallacy," by the great psychologists Amos
Tversky and Dani Kahneman (again) in 1983, it's clear that we humans are
prone to make rather innocuous errors when information presented at them
with stories. In their famous example, "Linda," they show vividly how this
conjunction fallacy finds form.

Basically, Tversky and Kahneman presented a story of a fictional character
Linda as followed: Linda is 31 years old, single, outspoken, and very bright.

She majored in philosophy. As a student, she was deeply concerned with
issues of discrimination and social justice, and also participated in
anti-nuclear demonstrations. The question that followed was: Which is more
probable? A) Linda is a bank teller. B) Linda is a bank teller and is active in the
feminist movement. What's your answer?

Anyone in their right mind would answer A) Linda is a bank teller because "
more probable" simply and straightforwardly means "more statistically
probable." For Linda to be a bank teller who is also active in the feminist
movement, she would have to be a bank teller first. So, Linda's being a bank
teller is more statistically more probable. Well, I am not sure what was your
answer but most people actually chose B) Linda is a bank teller and is active
in the feminist movement.

When asked why, those who chose B in a series of experiments that the two
psychologists had conducted promptly referred to the fact that Linda is a
bank teller and is active in the feminist movement sounded more like a
realistic answer based on the information about who she was and what she
did as a student and before she became a bank teller. In this case, the
additional information "i s active in the feminist movement" is the element of
emotional      "representativeness"   that    undermines     rational    statistical
probability.

Imagine if Linda is your former classmate and you do not know what Linda is
doing, and how easy it is for you to add those last few words: "I haven't see
Linda in a while but last time we met she was a bank teller -- and was
probably still active in the feminist movement." Bite your tongue, resist the
temptation to be complicit in an act of terrorism. All you actually know is that
she WAS a bank teller.

Because we like stories that are more than just facts -- like in this Linda's
story -- therefore it goes the same as how our mind (and mouth) would like
to interject more than what we actually know, and are capable of knowing. Be
careful of that.

There was time that I mistakenly sent two messages to my friends upon the
death of my dog Paolo -- one wrote "Paolo just passed away," and the other
was "Paolo was a dog that loved to eat everything," Lo and behold, I received
many messages from those friends who mourned the loss of my dearest dog
to obesity, obesity-induced diseases (such as diabetics) and obesity-induced
immobility. There're even gossips going around that I was a "pug's killer"
because I "constantly overfed" my lovely Paolo.

The truth was that Paolo was only allowed to eat only one meal a day of dog's
food only. I didn't even know whether, as a dog, he was considered
overweight.

All I wanted to say was that Paolo was dead and that I remember him well as
a lovely dog that loved to eat -- these two information had nothing to do with
one another but all my friends, though "conjunction fallacy," connected the
invisible dots brought to them by their proneness to see a story in the two
separated ideas. In fact, all of us should know the principles of non-violent
communication and converse freely, with good intentions, with one another.

In fact, there is a plethora of evidence showing that a transparent workplaces
that disdain and reject gossiping tend to strive and retain their productivity
while those who are not tend to collapse. In organizational psychology, for
instance, there is a plenty of research findings that show how a company
would be much better off when the employees feel as though they can voice
their concerns about their superiors freely and directly with their superiors.

But we all know that which hardly happens, most of us (if not all) go to
someone we like -- such as our friends or colleagues -- to voice our
discontent with our superiors, which, needless to say, became gossips with
the    tendency   to   spread   and   therefore   get   distorted    among       the
friends/colleagues of those friends/colleagues with whom you share your
initial discontent. The evidence is clear that this circulation of information
doesn't benefit any individuals in the situation let alone the organization.

The entrepreneur (and to me, a thinker) Ray Dalio, in fact, writes and speaks
at length and especially in his book Principles ) about how he owes the
culture of transparency the success of his company Brigdewater Associates.
Combating gossip with nonviolent communication So, what exactly is
nonviolent communication? I would suggest that you read Rosenberg's book,
which I believe, to date, to be the best psychological guide on how to
"communicate to live" in the world today.

Indeed, if we are all to follow his principles, we won't be engaging at all in
gossiping. That is to say, gossip is, in Rosenberg's book, one of the violent
methods of communication that his principles aim at preventing us from
engaging. In short, here're the four principles which have many overlaps with
what I have discussed earlier in this post (and please read the book!). When
communicating you thoughts: Only speak of your observation -- and refrain
from your evaluation.

Saying that your boss "talks too much" is the latter and won't solve any
problem, whereas saying that your boss "occasionally speaks of his personal
irrelevant personal experience during the meeting, extending the meeting's
time by 20-30 minutes, may be an issues to be discussed" could lead to an
action that helps your boss to change himself for better; Connect your
moralistic judgement (if you happen to have one) to how you feel.

For instance, instead of saying that "You are so stupid for saying that -- how
could you?" you might want to connect your immediate judgement (i.e.,
stupid) to the feeling that makes you feel that way, which could allow you to
put together something that also weaves in essential facts, for example,
"When you're talking specifically about this issue in a way that seems to imply
that you are not aware of the very news that most of us should know by ways
of our office's mass weekly e-newsletters, it makes me feel sad that we
haven't been able to make sure that all the important news have reached all
of us.

I'd like you to tell me how you feel when I tell you this"; Avoid adverbs such as
always, never, ever, frequently, and state the facts -- how often is a

statement such as "You never listen to me" is true?; and, Be explicit about
your request, and not your demand -- try to motivate others to want to do
things that are basic to human needs rather than demand that they do it.
Most people do not want to be demanded but welcome polite and, most
importantly, non-violent, requests.

Finally: Blame it on me! Again, I cannot emphasize enough that I disdain
gossiping. I do not trust any news and media that base their reports on
unfound rumors and especially gossips aiming at discrediting other people --
and so shouldn't you. Those who gossip always either uses pseudonym and
do not want their name to be revealed to any third parties.

How many times that we have heard someone about to engage in gossip
saying "Don't tell anyone that I am telling you this." It should be a
commonsense that one should always be super cautious when told anything
by anyone who does not want to be responsible for their own words. When
one do not have to be responsible for your own words, one can say whatever
one wants -- and that's a deeply, insanely, and outrageously dangerous thing.

According to this logic, not only should those words not be heard by a
sensible person (and I mean YOU) but they should also be condemned by
everyone who wants to live in a better society. I do not trust -- and therefore
I do not read anything -- written by anyone who does not want to be
responsible for their own words. I hope that you feel the same way.

I would not want to live in a society in which anyone could simply be a victim
of false accusation and gossips. I believe in justice. Hence, I believe that the
best way to bring anyone to justice is to stop speculating, and especially
circulating false accusations, and simply bring all the parties involved in the
accusation to a transparent investigation. It's always hurtful for me to hear
that it's so easy for people to make false accusations about others.

But since I am a fan of Steven Pinker, I will continue to believe that even that
which is the case, we are still living in the most peaceful time in the history of
mankind (although that doesn't and shouldn't mean that we should just sit

back and relax now). But hey, if those people still do not stop trying to get
you to listen to their tasteless gossip, I have another suggestion.

Say that although you'd like to be a good listener, you once read a blog post
that urged you to consider engaging with gossip unsafe even for innocent
individuals. Go ahead: blame me. You can even use my name.

     On Design Thinking Part 2: It All
            Began in Kraków

title: On Design Thinking Part 2: It All Began in Kraków
excerpt: SO, LET ME TELL YOU a bit about the background of this design thinking series. For most of 2016 and 2017, I had many opportunities to conduct workshops on this very topic, which I found to be speaking directly to the question that I had bee…
body:
SO, LET ME TELL YOU a bit about the background of this design thinking series.

For most of 2016 and 2017, I had many opportunities to conduct workshops
on this very topic, which I found to be speaking directly to the question that I
had been having for many years: How could I use years of theoretical
knowledge that I had learned from school in real life? Design thinking came
to me first as a surprise.

I, too, taught that it's one of those terms that people liked to name-drop
because "it sounded cool," without knowing its true meaning. Only after a
few months of a truly deep dive, I realized that this was a gold mine. In fact, I
personally had long been doubting my own ability to be an individual in the
world.

In many posts prior to this, I talked about how I felt that the educational and
socials system in which I was born were too rigid for m e, to the point that I,
at several points in my life, felt as though I did not have a lot to contribute to
my community. It's until I had recognized some of the key principles of
design thinking (about which I talked in my Day 76 post ) that I began to see
myself, retroactively, somewhat as a natural design thinker.

Hold on right there. I am not saying that which was a good thing. To be a
design thinker, as I wrote in my last post, one has to constantly challenge
himself/herself with the idea that there's always a possibility for things to be
better than it is. In other words, one has to see things the way they actually
are, and not through the lens of acceptance by the majority.

For instance, I saw I was a horrible good student throughout my formal
education simply because I didn't always good grades (actually, almost never)
and I had never won any awards at all. The society thought me as an ordinary
majority,    who      would     probably      go     on    to     become         a
much-less-than-extraordinary employee in a mediocre company.

That's the way things were presented to me through the lens of acceptance
by the majority -- so, what psychologists would call -- "social proof." Then, I
asked myself to take a look at the situation differently. The question I had for
myself was, What was the reason for me to feel no inspiration at all for what I
was studying? Hence, the bad grades and hence the award-less student life.
That's exactly the time when I realized that I was starting to see things the
way they were.

If anyone were to study what he/she was passionate in, there's very likely he
or she would do much better than. Wouldn't you agree? I knew that I enjoyed
making drawing. I liked to travel and look at great buildings. Most of the film
photographs I took back then were all buildings, monuments, and edifices.
Thinking about architecture, and how I could re- design my house to fit my
own customized need, made me feel really happy. Every day was a holiday
when I thought about architecture so to speak.

Of course, it would be nice if I was also good at biology (my worst subject in
high school), geography, mathematics, and chemistry. But maybe I wasn't
interested in those fields as much -- so why would I let things that I wasn't
even interested in define who I was? So, I shifted my focus from being "good
at what the society thought I should be good at"to what mattered to me and
my passion, and that which, back then, was architectural design.

I then decided to enroll in a modest technical institution where my entrance
examination score was just enough to be admitted outside of Bangkok.
Anyone who knew my academic attributes as a student would not be able to
predict how I would go on to receive a First Class Honors in architectural
design from the technical institute where I enrolled as an undergraduate
student. After that, as they always say, the rest is history. I did not know how
to call my way of thinking then.

So, my classmates helped me by calling it "thinking out of the box," "critical
thinking," "thinking laterally," and most amusingly, "thinking like Non." Years
have passed, and I have come to see design thinking in a much more
interesting spotlight. And now that I have a name to call it, I have become
much more interested in exploring what it has to offer. Design thinking
wasn't just a cliche, it's a combination of practical philosophy, cognitive
psychology and robust strategy for innovation.

My realization that I could just focus on what I was interested in came from
my (then quite) shallow understanding of the self improvement. But for sure
my ability to see things the way they were got me a jump start. Of course, my
love and obsession with both cognitive psychology and robust strategy for
innovation started much later (from early this year onward to be precise) so
these two fields, in fact, gradually grew on me as profound elements of
design thinking.

Many of my classmates back then still made fun of me by calling design
thinking "thinking like Non" -- they could never forget that, couldn't they?
So, to begin, I offered myself as a laborer and keen observer to a company
that used design thinking to innovate. Then, I read as many books as possible
on the topic.

I continued to search as many examples and case studies as I could online,
and in real life, to get the sense of what might be the practical gist of the idea:
To see what worked and what did not.

And most important, I learned from hundreds of students from all age
groups, academic levels, and placements in life in their professional career
paths not only what is the apt way to introducing this concept to them, but
also what might help them to become a better individual in whatever they
were trying to do. In the process that took more than a full year of a
combination of self-reflection, self-closing (so that I could think clearly), and
self-exploring, I indeed had learned quite a bit.

The real challenge that I had was when I decided to offer a course called
"design thinking" as a semester-long undergraduate course for non-designs
students at a new university where I would soon be lecturing as a visiting
instructor.This was supposed to be a sabbatical semester for me; so I was in
that mode of thinking as well (i.e., an "I can teach anything" mode) when I
almost carelessly gave the title of the course to the administrator of the
university I was a visiting instructor.

Well, normally a course that I offered were merely a two-day-long workshop,
so there're so much to instruct during the 16-20 hours of lectures,
workshops, and intense seminar. But a semester-long, as in 15-week long,
undergraduate course? For students who did not necessary have any
knowledge of academic or non-academic background in design? More
importantly, was I mad? In the spirit of design thinking, I took up this very
challenge anyway. I thought to myself: What if I could use design thinking to
"design" this course?

The course eventually had the following aspects: A 15-week-long course; for
each week, a reading introducing the key definition of "creativity" from a
variety of academic fields that are totally new to students is introduced to
the class; Every 2-3 weeks, students would be asked to engage in a hands-on
workshop designed to test their synthesis of those key aspects of creativity
ranging from a coffee cup to a gift-giving experience, to a better uncluttered
life; and, There's no final paper -- students would determine what they want
to do for their own good at the end of the course How did I come up with
this?

Let's look at the three principles that I wrote about in my last post and how I
used them to come up with a design thinking course at Jagiellonian
University in Krakow, Poland. First, don't always assume that the end-users
of your design know what they want. This was the most important factor for
my designing the course the way I did.

The classic joke goes, "You can always be sure that your boss will never see
what you bring for him to see if that thing is an innovation -- and you can
also always be sure that he will not like it right away because he won't know
how it works." My first idea was to teach design thinking as a design course,
and that was basically what students were looking forward to as they had
signed up for the course weeks earlier even before they met me in person
and received the preliminary syllabus!

If I were to give them that course, I would be giving them exactly "what they
thought creativity was." What's the point of learning about creativity if you
already know what it was? And to the point -- my own point -- what did I
know really about design that made me feel so confident to teach them? So,
that idea was only partially saved and the rest of it got immediately thrown
into the garbage can.

I realized that both me and my students were not any of kinds of design
experts.    I   knew   architectural   design,   but   not   industrial,   graphic,
communication, etc, design. So, I decided to offer the course with the most
basic sets of knowledge that would get the students attention throughout the
15-week-long semester -- the idea of creativity and innovation. Second, don't
always assume that things exist because they work. Far from it actually.

The reason why things "look like they work" has to do, first and foremost,
with our "availability bias" and "social proof." I cannot recall how many times I
heard people's saying "That building is great; they're so many people there so
it must be a good building." So, the assumption is that because a lot of people
are using it, so it must be good; and/or, because the definition of a building is
something that doesn't collapse, so it is standing and people are using it, so
therefore it must be a good building.

This is not all wrong, but definitely not all right. Most of the time, we use
whatever that is available to us. In Bangkok, I drink instant coffee -- although
I know that brew coffee is much more delicious and better for my health
(there're many research that show that the process by which the instant
coffee grain is made dried is unhealthy). But it is not that easy to find a brew
coffee where I live in Bangkok, so I drink whatever I can find! Instant coffee
does works!

And if a lot of coffee-lovers like me feel the same way, we would all be buying
up instant coffee from the supermarket, which should not be confused with
the impression that "Bangkok people like instant coffee" or that "Instant
coffee is good." O.K.

So, this example is easy to understand because of the comparative
benchmark that I have (i.e., I have drunk better brew coffee elsewhere in the
world), so that which is the reason why I know that instant coffee exist not
because it was the best, but because it was the best viable option in a
particular circumstance. Now, I want you to take a step further -- by imaging
that everything around you can be better.

Look at everything around you; a pen, a notebook, computer, mobile phone,
etc., and asked yourself, "If you were to design the world's first pen,
notebook, computer, mobile phone -- as if they have never existed before in
the history of mankind -- how would you go about and design them and what
will they look like? This was a challenging question that I also asked myself
when I was putting together this design thinking course.

So, instead of hitting the internet and search as many available and existing
syllabi for courses with a similar name -- and perhaps learn from those in
designing my own course -- I started with finding a nice spot in my
apartment's compound where I could see the trees, smell the glass, and listen
to the singing of the birds. These were the elements that made my apartment
in Poland sort of a heaven on earth even though no other amenities were
there.

Then, I visualized how should creativity be taught. By doing so, I wrote this
following paragraph that became the basis of this course: This is an
experimental class. There are three reasons behind its experimental nature.
First, the idea of "studying creativity" seems like an oxymoron. A fixed set of
conventions may provide guidelines for what might be useful to learn but it
should not overshadow the creative process that learners should design for
themselves.

Second, as design is not about thinking about what to design, but one step
beyond in not only conceiving but also producing it to test out in reality, the
