to keep your efficiency up, let alone keep your hope and faith in humanity.

Now that I think about it again (more carefully this time) the urge to answer
this question actually never came to me earlier than today. It's simply
because I always like to spend time improving myself, rather than bringing
other people down. Sadly, there are many people who find pleasure in doing
so. Just today I often to overheard a conversation between two strangers.
While the first one was obviously very proud of his recent job promotion, the
other one was doing the best he could to bring him down.

I heard, "Someone else got a better promotion." I honestly did not
understand why the second person (I assume was a "colleague" or "friend")
could not just be happy with the first one. Again, I only knew very little about
what's going between these two. The one who got promoted didn't talk back,

walk away. Instead, he smiled at everything that the second person had to
say. Well, maybe there's a point there. Maybe they're true friends.

Maybe there's a point in having a true friend like that to keep your ego in
check. As a trained anthropologist (and still trying so hard to be a practicing
Buddhist), I also rarely judge other people. One very very simple reason: I
simply don't have the time to judge everything and everyone. My mother gets
annoyed at me when I couldn't help her decide which shirt she would wear to
go out, or which food to eat for dinner. I simply do not have any opinion.

I simply don't have the willpower to judge which is better. Either, or even
both, is okay. There're so many things in the world I would like to judge, such
as the quality of great non- fiction writings (yes, I am a nerd so I read about
200 non-fiction books a year), or the importance of acclaimed architectural
edifices and how they shape how we think about the environment.

I see people's making judgment on someone or something based news they
hear from the television, based on from gossip they eavesdropped from a
stranger, and based on from flaw sources. These are the sources that they
themselves would not make any important decisions based on. It's only so
ironic how these people are so comfortable making judgment about others
based on these sources that they themselves would not rely on.

Psychologically speaking, the kinds of things one can make a "snap
judgement" about those that "confirm" your already established (however
credible) assumption. This kind of snap judgement is called "heuristics." It's a
mental shortcut that most of us use to make decisions (although, not all
decisions are judgments, and not all judgements are equal). Clearly I can
make a quick decision about wearing black rather than red today.

I have to make a judgment which shirt to wear but it's not a big decision, and
whichever option that I choose probably won't lead to a calamitous result (as
long as I use my now-favourite Issey Miyake Nuit D'Issey perfume). That said,
this snap decision is barely a good one. Almost always, we're already biased
by our lack of something.

An example: If you can't afford an iPhone and have recently heard from
someone -- just one person -- about a new iPhone's being bad, you're likely
to think that the iPhone isn't worth your hard-earned cash.

Psychologically, you are being "pre-suaded" (Dr Bob Caidini's term) to
"pre-judge" the new iPhone by your own pre-existing "confirmation bias."
Just to complicates things a bit: If somehow someone happens to tells you
that the iPhone really isn't good, you're very very (as in 87.5%) likely to
confirm your judgement that the iPhone really really isn't good -- even
though you have never touched it, never seen it, and all you know is the name
of the product and the fact that "two people in the world" have said it isn't
good.

How strange. That is to say, that "lack of something" is the pathological
symptom of jealousy. If you ever notice yourself, whenever you feel the urge
to accuse, blame, or make someone wrong, it's almost always the case that
you're jealous of that person. Hold on. I know what you're thinking about:
Your sudden "snap decision" to disagree with the statement. You might say,
"But I blame my inferior everyday, clearly as a superior, there's nothing that
the inferior could have that I don't." Muse over that for a second.

That feeling of lack doesn't have anything to do with rank; but with
appearance, age, personality, or even the ability to be carefree with life for
which you, a superior, always yearn but do not have. As all the Stoic
philosophers like to say when someone is accusing you, "Be proud; nobody
accuse, blame, or gossip about a dead dog." I have written about this earlier
but let me repeat it again. In dealing with people's accusing, blaming, and
making you wrong, just rise above.

You have something that they do not have, and that's why they're trying to
bring you down. What I usually do is this two golden steps: Forgive and
forget. You'd rather enjoy yourself -- be in the moment -- than dignifying
jealousy.

Judging others -- let alone accusing or blaming -- based on information
confirming your bias is nothing less of judging the quality of the entire Indian
Ocean by the small cup of water you have just scooped up from a small
contaminated river two thousand miles away from that Ocean. As I have
written earlier, one of my 12 rules for life is to be content with not having to
have an opinion on things about which I do not know.

This goes without saying that I am a big fan of Dr Jordan B Peterson's "set
your house in perfect order before criticizing the world" rule. And since a
"perfect order" is rarely accomplishable, don't criticize. Live your life in the
here and the now.

Back to Tolle's words "If you are very alert, very present, you can do so
without ego involvement — without blaming, accusing, or making the other
wrong." In short, I find the act of badmouthing other people to be
intellectually, spiritually and morally a big fat waste of time. One rule still
applies: Don't engage, walk away.

                           On Freedom

title: On Freedom
excerpt: FREEDOM IS ONE OF THE most difficult topics. We all want freedom, but... Freedom from what? Apparently, freedom, at least in the society in which we are living today, doesn't mean you can do whatever you want. That would be like living in w…
body:
FREEDOM IS ONE OF THE most difficult topics. We all want freedom, but...

Freedom from what? Apparently, freedom, at least in the society in which we
are living today, doesn't mean you can do whatever you want. That would be
like living in what the philosopher Thomas Hobbes describes as the "state of
nature" where everyone can use force to take whatever they want from
anyone. Apparently, too, nobody living in the "free world" today would accept
the idea of any individual freedom's to hurt others.

What about the freedom to hurt yourself? Well, no country in the world
legalizes suicide for a reason. Suicide is a "conscious decision" that only
someone with a somewhat developed mental capacity could perform. What
this means is that the society to which that someone belongs must have had
invested some resources in bringing him up. Suicide, that is to say, is both a
waste of those resources, and an "immoral" act that can potentially mislead
the rest of the society.

What about the freedom to be stupid and hurt yourself indirectly? That's up
for a debate. But even that, some of the most hardcore "libertarians" (i.e.,
those who believe in the doctrine of absolute free will) have increasingly
endorsed the idea of "libertarian paternalism" -- the 2017 Nobel Laureate
Richard H. Thaler and the author and legal scholar Cass R. Sunstein' s idea

that even those hardcore "free-willers" to live a better life.

Even these hardcore free-willers want to be told that fastening their seat
belts will prevent them from preventable death, and that they should
probably not smoke too much because smoking kills. So, freedom to live
trumps to the freedom to live in the dark. These hardcore free-wellers may
even argue that they're "living even more freely" because they are accepting
the "forced better way of life," which they otherwise wouldn't have known.

I am personally particularly partial to the very last idea (i.e., "libertarian
paternalism" in case you missed it), as it speaks to my own being. I, too,
believe in free will, but I also want to say, please help me if you can tell me
how to live a better life; please tell me what's bad for my health; and, please,
please, please, tell me what I should do to live a better life. For instance,
although I have a PhD in anthropology, I don't know much about food
sciences.

So, even as a free-willer, I often beg (through voting for good people) my
government to ban junk food, and endorse nutritious stuff. I also support
banning of cigarettes at all public places (even though I was once a chronic
smoker). I don't want to die young!

In case I wasn't clear what I meant earlier (since "libertarian paternalism"
may sound oxymoronic), here's the passage from Thaler and Sunstien's best-
seller Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness: The
libertarian aspect of our strategies lies in the straightforward insistence that,
in general, people should be free to do what they like—and to opt out of
undesirable arrangements if they want to do so.

To borrow a phrase from the late Milton Friedman, libertarian paternalists
urge that people should be "free to choose." We strive to design policies that
maintain or increase freedom of choice. When we use the term libertarian to
modify the word paternalism, we simply mean liberty-preserving. And when
we say liberty-preserving, we really mean it. Libertarian paternalists want to
make it easy for people to go their own way; they do not want to burden

those who want to exercise their freedom.

What this mean is that even the smartest people among the free-willers still
need some advice. There're two reasons for that. First, a society must be
defended in the world where knowledge is, simply, too abundant for a single
person to know it all. The best of us have to tell the rest what to do -- that
which is least interruptive and most benefitting to our lives and livelihood.
Second, there're plenty of evidences that show complete freedom could lead
to chaos. Think of a traffic jam, for instance.

Or, air pollution. Sound familiar? We all have the right to buy private cars and
emit some pollution to the air, but too much of that would suffocate the
entire society -- including your own children. As we have leaned from great
thinker like Garett Hardin, a society can also be overwhelmed with negative
things if we're all thinking we're free to do whatever we want. Hardin calls it
"a tragedy of the commons," which I think is an urgent question of every
society today.

This is when and where "libertarian paternalism" comes in. You can be the
smartest person in a certain field, but you can't be the smartest in all. So, if
we assume that our government is capable of recruiting the "smartest"
people from all the fields to help us live better in all domains of life, then
we're all "libertarian paternalists" in the sense that we allow ourselves to be
led by those who (at least we think they do) know what they are doing. But, is
that freedom?

Is freedom then simply the ability to follow the rules of the society that
presumably knows how to recruit the best to tell us what to do? What about
some of those great ideas, say, "civil disobedience?" What if I don't agree with
the idea of fastening a seat belt? What if I think banning cigarettes is stupid?
Can I disobey things I don't agree? Shouldn't that be considered a greater
form of freedom? That's both a great and horrible question. In a way, it's also
great to be fallibilistic.

That said, since we now know the usefulness of seat belts (and danger of
smoking), the idea of disobeying the fasten seat belt rule seems, well, plainly
stupid. But hey, maybe I could be wrong (that's fallibilism's talking).. So, let's
look at the bigger picture. Should the libertarian paternalists themselves, too,
have some doctrine on which to base their argument?

The philosopher Immanuel Kant argues that freedom is (and I of course
paraphrase) "the ability to live by your own rules." If you have a set of rules by
which you want to live your life, and nobody can possibly entice you to follow
otherwise then, according to Kant, you're a "free person." Yes, simply put: If
you're a desert-hater, for instance, you're only free if you will always turn
down an offer of a chocolate cake after the dinner at any cost.

That which means that you have own your rule by which you live. But what if
the society forces you to eat that chocolate cake? If obeying your own rule
means disobeying that of the society's, then you're only free if you engage in
an act of civil disobedience. Yes, not eating that chocolate cake and being put
in jail is freedom, for Kant. In addition to avoiding decision fatigue, there's
another true socio-psychological benefit of turning down that slice of
chocolate cake.

I resort, again, to Rolf Dobelli's The Art of the Good Life": By being consistent,
you signal where you stand and establish the areas where there's no room for
negotiation. You communicate self-mastery, making yourself less vulnerable
to attack. Mutual deterrents during the Cold War were based largely on this
effect. The USA and the USSR both knew that a nuclear strike would mean
instant retaliation. No deliberation, no situational weighing up of pros and
cons. The decision for or against the red button had already been taken.

Pressing it first simply wasn't an option. Kant, however, only argues that
those rules have to be strictly adhered to his strictest "categorical
imperative," or the rules that could well "become universal" (apparently
eating a chocolate cake wouldn't be one of those). Think of race and racism,
for example. Wouldn't you agree that we should be all treated equal despite
the color of our skin?

If your answer, like mine, is yes; then you get the point; that which could
become a universal law and therefore is a "categorical imperative." The
Reverend Martin Luther King Jr. believed that the "the rights of citizens to
political and social freedom and equality" should be universal; hence, he went
on to, one and for all, change the course of history by civil disobeyed the rule
of the state, of the society, and of the conventionality, of the time in which he
lived. That's just one obvious example.

There're plenty. For these, the philosopher Immanuel Kant came up with a
few, such as don't kill yourself, don't lie, and don't waste your capacity and
learned skills.

I am also sure you can agree that you shall not kill thyself, and that you
probably should not lie (e.g., one of Dr Jordon Peterson's antidotes to chaos
"Tell the truth, or at least don't lie" ), but what about "waste your capacity and
skill?" Well, if all medical doctors, who spent years learning how to be
medical doctors, have decided that they all no longer want to be in the
profession, what would happen to those ill people who need their "capacity
and skill?" Surely for a society to train a medical doctor, some resources
would have to be invested on such training.

So, for Kant, a free society can't operate if it only trains people who will end
up wasting their capacity and skill (watch out those who're not working in
what you've been trained to do!). So, it seems as though there isn't a lot of
ways to be completely free. Now, I want to turn 360 degrees back to the
Stoics, Buddha (and John Lennon). What exactly do these great practical
minds think we give ourselves the freedom from? Let me give you 4 of them.

If you can be free from all of these, you are free: Freedom from the ability to
think. The Stoics are of grave concern with our mental ability to overthink
and to, plainly, not stop thinking. The author and and spiritual leader Ekhart
Tolle argues in The Power of Now that most of us wasting our opportunity to
enjoy life by thinking about the past and the future -- and therefore forsaking
the presence.

The singer, writer, and artists John Lennon, sort of said the same thing that,
"Life is what happens to you while you're busy making other plans." Descartes
might be right that our ability to think (i.e., "I think") gives us the sense of the
existence (i.e., "therefore I am.").

What he's wrong (or perhaps didn't address quite eloquently, to give the
great philosopher the benefit of the doubt) is that this ability to think doesn't
give us the pleasure that we as a species should enjoy -- the pleasure of
"being in the moment." How many of us hear voices in our heads almost all
the time? The answer is 99.9999% Most of us find it hard to stop thinking.

When we think, as Ekhart Tolle argues, "we stop living in the presence,"
which is a waste of the opportunity to be happy. The real freedom to have is
the freedom to have that "switch" that you can turn it on and off whenever
you wish (This takes a lot of practice and I am still working on it); Freedom
from judging others. It's easy for us to be judgmental and opinionated. We're
living in the world where information is treated as social capital.

This world makes us want to be judgmental and opinionated just about
everything. The same way you wouldn't want your name to be associated
with crime; you wouldn't want to cast your vote on events, actions, news, of
which you do not have a complete knowledge. The real freedom is the ability
to restrain yourself from jumping to quick to a conclusion, just because it
confirms one of your preexisting biases, or because you feel the social
pressure to appear "smart," or because you feel jealous of others.

Simply say no to things that you do not have enough knowledge on which to
form an opinion. I do that all the time, and, needless to say, it feels super
liberating every time. The true freedom lies in your ability to, simply, be a
"gentleperson" in a society that is already rather hateful, rough, and chaotic;
Freedom from wanting to control what is outside of your control. The Stoics
have said it all. There're only two things that you can control: Your own value
and your own opinions.

Nobody can take these two things away from you and you always have the
absolute control over them. Problems often appear when we begin to think
that we can control other people.

Parents' wanting to control their teenage children -- because they think it's
in the freedom and right to do so -- often end in misery because they choose
to place their bet on what they can't control instead of being fully in charge
of their own opinion about their children's ability to freely go out in the
world (and therefore make their own mistakes to learn). You are not free
when you want to control what you can't control. Freedom from seeking
revenges.

One of my older colleagues used to remind me that "revenge is a dish best
served cold." He also consistently tells everyone he could find the "famous
story" of how he managed to systematically undermine and destroy his
former boss' research center, and therefore his boss' entire career. This older
colleague argued that it was his book who "tried to destroy his career first,"
so it's was in this older colleague's right to seek revenge.

This older colleague always spoke of this revenge as an "achievement" -- "I
served it to him not just cold, but coldest." I never agreed with this older
colleague of mine when we're still colleagues, and I still don't. I feel as though
this older colleague of mine had never moved on from the past -- he's still
living that past over and over. And since when destroying someone's career
something to be proud of anyway?

As both the Buddhist and Chinese old proves go, "Revenge is likely lead to
another revenge." For Buddha, the act of seeking revenge itself yields deep
visceral anger that surpasses multiple folds in magnitude the temporary joy
received through seeing someone suffers through it. That joy, however, is not
the one that gives you the sense of tranquility, but instability. Whenever I
think about the mistakes of seeking revenge in the past, I always think my
heart's beating heavy -- and it's not a joyous one.

The shrewd and wise Stoics never allow us to dwell over revenge. It's a waste
of time. There're many other things more important to do. Seneca, for
instance, never tried to seek revenge from those who banished, or even
sentenced with him hard labour. He simply focused on how to improve
himself and get back into the game. He's not insecure in any ways.

As a result, Seneca's never seeking revenge earned him the reputation of a
true sage, and those who did many bad things to him all voluntarily came
down to his knees to learn from him. In other words, a truly free person think
of revenge "as a dish best not served." Forgive and forget. Am I not being free
by not using my capacity and skill that I have been trained to do? I sort of
agree with Kant.

Although, as a trained architect who barely designs any buildings, I also think
that we should all have the right to change our profession. My decision to
become an architect (at 16) wasn't a wise one; and hey, maybe I am just a
horrible architect whose absence of buildings is better for the society as a
whole. My architectural training actually never went away.

So, as I was being trained to become an anthropologist over the past 8 years
of my life, I kept my previous profession at bay and tried to incorporate it to
my anthropological training in any possible ways that I could. The point is
not to retreat to a complete solitude. That wouldn't be a "free will" but a lazy
(and perhaps a selfish) thing to do, for Kant. So, the fact that I am still here
writing this, it's fine. It's still considered freedom.

I am a writer and an anthropologist. I am still an architect, who now happens
to know quite a bit about anthropology.

post Day 94: On Making an Argument Sat, 27 Sep 2025 00:00:00 +0000 O.k. I
just couldn't sleep. I have been having an issue with sleeping lately. I perhaps
should seriously take my mother's advice not to take a nap after my
late-afternoon run. Now, I am wide awake. Even after using the secret
military technique whose claim is that it could make anyone fall asleep in two
minutes, I still could not fall asleep.

So, while rolling on one side of the bed (as I had this hypothesis that changing
from sleeping on the back to the side helps me fall asleep; spoiler alert: I was
wrong), I came up with an idea for this post. So, I want to write about
arguments. Specifically, I want to write about how to have the kinds of
arguments that benefit you and your counterpart. First and foremost, the
rule of thumb is not to argue with anyone.

This is one of the late life and self-help guru Dale Carnegie's golden rules in
How to Win Friends and Influence People, and also, well, a commonsense.
Arguing for the sake of arguing is a waste of time. So, should you argue, make
sure that which is a productive and friendly exchange of diverging or
opposite views that has the potential to lead to intellectual benefits of the
involved parties.

Countless of time in my own life and of those whom I care, I witnessed how
an argument could lead, and have led, to an end of friendship, relationship,
co- workership, etc. Those are the instances whereby not having an
argument at all would have been better for the parties involved. Again, an
argument that leads to an end of a friendship isn't worth pursuing. But, why
does it happen? So, let's get it straight rule by rule.

The most important rule: Not just an argument (but especially for an
argument) but any form of communication that is likely to be effective has to
be nonviolent. I am not talking just about a direct verbal abuse (which is
obviously violent), but any form of ineffective communication, such as an
criticism, judgment, sarcasm, and vague statement. It's true that most of us
get emotional when we engage in an argument, and forget that it's never as
important as the relationship you have with your counterpart with whom
you're arguing.

So, for sure, the first thing to do is to watch your own emotion and never let
it override your reason. The methods of nonviolent communication will help
you do just that. I wrote about this a few times in the past, but let's
reemphasize it here again.

[gallery ids="4825,4826,4827" type="rectangular"] Like me, the psychologist
Dr Marshall B Rosenberg believes that the main reason for most conflicts we
have in the world today is our inability to communicate "non-violently."
When we communicate, we don't just say what we feel and what we need
from those to whom we're expressing ourselves. We judge, compare,
criticize, curse -- most of the time without any good reason. Most of the
time, argues Dr Rosenberg, we use imprecise, judgmental and presumptive
language to prematurely evaluate a situation.

These are communication that involves the use of negative adjectives (e.g.,
bad, mean, evil) and unnecessary adverbs (e.g., always, never, frequently)
which are neither useful to get our meaning across, nor to get the other
people to empathize with you. For instance, how many times does saying
"You're always late" help to encourage someone to be punctual?

Here's      one   of   the   examples   from   Dr's   Rosenberg's    Nonviolent
Communication: A Language of Life: Life-Changing Tools for Healthy
Relationships: A couple in distress attending a workshop provides an
additional    illustration   of   how   nonspecific   language    can    hamper
understanding and communication. "I want you to let me be me," the woman
declared to her husband. "I do!" he retorted. "No, you don't!" she insisted.

Asked to express herself in positive action language, the woman replied, "I
want you to give me the freedom to grow and be myself." Such a statement,
however, is just as vague and likely to provoke a defensive response.

She struggled to formulate her request clearly, and then admitted, "It's kind
of awkward, but if I were to be precise, I guess what I want is for you to smile
and say that anything I do is okay." Often, the use of vague and abstract
language can mask oppressive interpersonal games. The expert on marriage
the psychologist John Gottman at the University of Washington also adds
that one secret to a long lasting marriage is precisely the use of precise,
non-judgmental and non-presumptive language.

For instance, if your partner forgets to take the garbage out, it's because he
"might have simply forgotten to do so" and not because he was "bad," "lazy,"
or "taking advantage of you." Asking your partner whether he forgot to take
the garbage out is likely to receive a good response than rhetorically asking
whether he is doing it intentionally because he is "bad," "lazy", or "taking
advantage of you." Once you get to the very bottom of what you're trying to
express every time before you're doing so, it's likely that you won't
communicate violently.

Alright. So, a nonviolent communication is the undergirding structure of all
good arguments. Here're the rules (I am cheating here by taking the first 4
rules from Dr Jordan B Peterson's 12 Rules for Life ): Treat yourself like
someone you are responsible for helping. What Dr Peterson means here is
that many times we treat other people better than we treat ourselves. For
instance, I have friends who are always there for me when I need them, but
whenever they need me they never call.

They don't want to bother me. I think that's unfair. Dr Peterson's example is
that we tend to make sure that our pet (e.g., dog, cat) take prescribed
medicines, while we tend to forget to take our own medicines. We're kinder
to those about whom we care. In making an argument, you need to be as kind
to yourself as your counterpart. You're also learning from the process and
the argument should enrich, not deteriorate, your relationship with your
counterpart.

Don't pursue an argument that is not helping you personally; Assume that the
person you are listening to might know something you don't.

I must have heard this from one of the podcasts I listened to a while ago, but
there's this line that really sticks: "If you can't first argue in the position of
your opposition, you have no right to argue your own." What this rule will
remind you is that while making an argument, watch out of things that might
allow you to excel in your understanding of the subject matter. Again, the
point of the argument is not to win, but to learn.

By why do we think that the purpose of an argument is to win? In Metaphors
We Live By, the linguistic psychologists George Lakoff and Mark Johnson
argue that it's because of how we have been using "war metaphors," such as
"Your claims are indefensible," " He attacked every weak point in my
argument," "His criticisms were right on target," to name a few (and I
recommend the book; it's a classic!). You get the idea. But that's misleading.

Once and for all, we should treat arguments, not as war, but as learning
process. Many of us today are trying to re-write these kinds of misleading
metaphors. Love, for instance, doesn't have to something that we would "fall
into," "hurt by" or cause "heartbreak." Using these as metaphors for love
really isn't helping; Tell the truth – or, at least, don't lie. Yepppppp; Be precise
in your speech. Yep. Again vague and imprecise speeches, according to Dr
Rosenberg, are considered violent communication.

When you want something to do something (e.g., take the trash out), be
precise in your speech -- in this case, what should be done (e.g, "Could you
please take the trash out," not "Why is the trash still in the house"). In an
argument too, be clear in what you're trying to convey; Do some research, or
don't. Make no compromise here. An argument is only worth pursuing when
the two opposite views are based on some research.

There's one instance whereby I found myself engaging in a debate about the
economy of recycling with a colleague. It took us 5 minutes to realize that
none of us knew anything about that topic; hence, we just laughed at our
silliness and dropped the "argument. You can have a casual nonviolent
conversation about something to stir up some thoughts (but not gossip
though) but do not take it as an argument if none of you have done any
research or know anything about the subject matter.

As the Stoics would say, "it's completely okay to not having an opinion on
something." If the reason why you'd rather go one restaurant over the other
has to do only with your gut feeling, just say so; don't try to make up other
justifications for it; "When the facts change, I change my mind. What do you
do, sir? This quote is often credited to the great economist John Maynard

Keynes (despite the lack of any written evidence).

Keynes said so with regards to his changing view regarding macroeconomics
of the UK. Often, economists stick to their guns, and their theories; hence,
for someone as important as Keynes to say this, it's not to be taken lightly.
He's right. We don't know everything. Do you remember when we all used to
think that smoking was okay? The psychologist Dr Robert B Cialdini, in fact,
points that "consistency" is one of the tricks that salespeople use to sell us
things that aren't that worthy.

That's another golden rule (so many of them, lol). When engaging in an
argument, the only preparation to have is to be enlightened, to learn from it,
and to change your mind if you find the argument opposing you more
convincing. Avoid using the word "you" and try to use the word "I." The worse
kind of fallacy is the one that is directed against a person rather than the
position (i.e., ad hominem, in Latin lit. "to the person").

Using "I" makes it much easier to express feelings, and much harder to attack
the person.

While it's usually useful to start an argument with "I," e.g., "I believe," "I think,"
"I feel" -- and you must follow it up with the magic word, "because." The
psychologist Ellen Langer wrote in her classic study conducted in 1978 about
the magical power of the word "because." Well, it's not that magical, but let's
say that it would increase substantially the logical compliance and therefore
the chance of your argument's being heard and taken seriously. O.k. I am still
not sleepy.

How about a poster from the old time.

            In Praise of Incrementalism

title: In Praise of Incrementalism
excerpt: I AM JUST FIVE DAYS -- five days -- away from the golden "100 days" mark. For those who don't know why that mark is important, please return to the first page of this blog. I have been asked by a dozen of friends, "What are you going to do …
body:
I AM JUST FIVE DAYS -- five days -- away from the golden "100 days" mark. For

those who don't know why that mark is important, please return to the first
page of this blog. I have been asked by a dozen of friends, "What are you
going to do once you get to the hundredth day?" I actually have not thought
about that.

I am thinking of two options: Going back to my previous posts and revise
them (as in, "re- broadcast" or "radio replay"), and keep going beyond the
hundredth day. As of now, I prefer the first option a bit more because I feel
the urge to go back to my previous posts to read about my childish and
immature thoughts. My hope is to use my humility to answer whether or not
I have changed from those childish and immature days.

So, for the next five posts, I want to write about some the "key ideas" that
make me who I am today. I, however, will skip those you already know from
my previous posts, such as rebelliousness, lateral thinking, and morality. Oh,
and probably romance and sex as well. I will probably come back to these at
some points, but for the last 5 posts of the first 100 days, I want to talk about
something less subjective, and more scientifically proven to be the case for
self-development.

Let's start with the first one: I believe in consistent and gradual change, one
step at a time. Some may call it "incrementalism." I hold this principle dearest
because it has been the single most important principle that has so far
positively changed my life. In my previous post, I wrote about those 5,000
hours over 6 years that "got" me into learning from English textbooks.

Long story short, I didn't have any friends when I was in college; so, by
default, I had to find other way to spend my time when I wasn't in classes. I
ended up spending hours every single day for 6 years reading books in the
library -- since books could not really say whether they would like me to be
their friends (I should say "a friend," rather than friends, during my first year
in college because I managed to finish only one book during my freshman
year).

Yep, I didn't become a serial reader right away (now I can't stand not reading
something awesome everyday). Back then, I didn't write a single word. It's
simply because I could not even construct a simple grammatical sentence in
English (e.g., "Me loving you," which sounds like what Cookie Monster would
say). During the entire first year in college, therefore, I only managed to read
just one hundred-page-long book. Yes, just one book for the whole year
during my freshman year.

In 1999, it took me about three days to get through a page, on average. It was
that slow. But the process was not as annoying as I thought it would be. I still
remember the joy of flipping through my old paper dictionary looking for
words. That's what happened when you had to look up every single word in a
paper dictionary. But bit by bit, I was able to speed up my ability to
comprehend what went through my eyes.

Incrementally, bit by bit, one step at a time, I became faster at reading. Six
years later, in 2005, I could finish a book in a week. "Little by little" is the
meaning of "incrementalism." Just for one task -- the task to learn how to
read in English -- it took me six years to do it. In 2018, I can probably read a
book a day. I have come quite a long way from then -- little by little.

But I am already at least some 20 years away from those days (I graduated
college in 2004), so "incrementally," I could reduce my reading time from one
year to one day. That's 365 times faster -- but over the course of 20 years.
This year, for instance, I hope to finish about 200 books. It's still not 365
books because I have other things that I'd like to do as well.

In fact, the author Rolf Dobelli writes in The Art of the Good Life that we
should be more selective in how we read according to science. We're reading
wrong. We're reading neither selectively nor thoroughly enough. We let our
attention off the leash as though it were a dog we're happy to let roam,
instead of directing it toward its splendid quarry. We fritter our most
valuable resource on things that don't deserve them. Read a book twice? Why
not?

In music we're used to listening to a track more than once. And if you play an
instrument, you'll know you can't master a score on the first attempt. It takes
several concentrated iterations before you can hurry on to the next piece.
Why shouldn't the same go for books? The effect of reading twice isn't twice
the effect of reading once. It's much greater—judging by my own experience,
I'd put it at a factor of ten.

If I retain three percent of the content after one reading, after two readings
it's up to thirty percent. So don't start stamping your reading ticket until
you're about thirty, but be ruthless thereafter. Once you hit thirty, life's too
short for bad books. Since I am already 36, perhaps I should listen to Dobelli.
Yes, I think the remaining time my life is too short for bad books (and bad
people, bad news, and every bad thing in general).

I guess what I am trying to say here is that my goal to read 200 books this
year (and not 365) is because it's not about "how many" books that matter.
Just because I can read fast doesn't mean I have to read a lot. I can spend
more time doing other things, read the same book twice, and so on. [gallery
ids="4834,4835"      type="rectangular"]     Researches   have   shown       that
incrementalism is actually what makes great minds.

We humans are built to crave exciting story, so we like to hear stories that
are dramatic and magical. The author Malcolm Gladwell has written the
whole book about it in Outliers: The Story of Success (which I have written
about on this blog). But the truth is, as Gladwell points out in his book, even
Bill Gates, Thomas Edison, Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart and the members of
the Beatles needed those 10,000 hours to make them great -- incrementally.
The author Stephen J. Dubner writes about incrementalism.

What do Renaissance painting, civil-rights movements, and Olympic cycling
have in common? In each case, huge breakthroughs came from taking tiny
steps. In a world where everyone is looking for the next moonshot, we
shouldn't ignore the power of incrementalism. So, hey, let's do these three
things. First, let's enjoy what we are doing on the daily basis -- learning
things incrementally. The key here is to be consistent. Since there's no
moonshot, one has to keep pacing.

If you want to read well and write well, for goodness' sake just read and write
every day whenever you can -- on the bus, while waiting for the doctor, etc.
These two words: Be consistent. I still remember during my time as an
architecture student, I had a mantra which I strictly follow: " Never live a day
without a line." I find that, if you are living in the presence, this process of
learning incrementally is actually quite enjoyable if not liberating.

That's how all great artists, experts, minds becomes who they were/are.
Second, since the way our brain learns is incremental, incremental learning is
almost the only way to learn anything to be great. So, let's not kid ourselves
that any of us can be great at anything by being born into it. According to
science, hard work inhabits us with the habit(s) that allow(s) us to naturally
navigate activities in our lives. To put it simply, if you ain't working hard
enough, you ain't going to be good or great.

The next question is: What do you want to be? If you want to be good, then
you just learn incrementally. As you learn, you'll be more skillful at the task
you're learning. It may take weeks, months or years, but undoubtedly you'll
become better at that task. You can even slack off from time to time (we all

have a job, right?) If you want to be great, however, you have to push yourself
to be consistent about learning incrementally.

You might have to quit your other jobs to focus on learning incrementally on
what you want to be great at. You may have to devote 10,000 hours (that's
about 10 years of your waking hours) mastering that skill. It won't be easy but
it will be fun. There's no other way around, according to science at least.
You'll be better than good -- you'll be great, but there won't be any guarantee
that you will get the results or praises.

Again, you may not confuse being great at what you do (as in, better than
yourself the day before) and being at the top of the world (as in, getting all
the accolades). As a Stoic might advise, you can control how great do you
want to be, but you can't control how great others think you are.

So, if you choose to take this route, you must also take a Jordan B
Petersonian attitude of "Compare yourself to who you were yesterday, not to
who someone else is today"; Third, perhaps we can also learn to lower our
expectations. Since our brain allows us to learn in small increments, we
might be able to save ourselves from disappointments when expecting
ourselves or others to improve drastically. Only a month ago, I couldn't stand
being on a treadmill for more than 20 minutes. I found it absolutely boring.

Today, I can do one hour (with a 2 minute break in between). Come on, it's a
great achievement for me, okay? I gradually increased my time on the
treadmill by an increment of 5, so I began with 15 minutes, then 20, 25, 30,
and so on. I am a cycling freak so I used to have to drive for one hour
one-way to a dedicated cycling lane (which wasn't great for the environment)
in order to ride for an hour.

Now I can just do that at the comfort of the fitness center in my apartment
compound. Incrementalism rocks! Now, what else? Let's say, since I said in
the beginning of this post that incrementalism has undergirded my life.
Here're the top few. Finance: From an ATM, I always withdraw a bit less than
I want. For instance, if my plan is to get $100, I would just get $90.

Since, to me, I don't see a lot of difference between $100 and $90, but after
ten withdrawals, those $10 will accumulate to a hundred. It's like getting a
$100 cash back every tenth withdrawal. Isn't that kind of nice? This goes with
my saving plans and so on.

Incrementalism is great because you don't even notice it (and that's one of
the argument that got the founder of the field of behavioral economics
Richard Thaler the Nobel Prize!) Food consumption: That too, I always eat
until I was about 70% full. Since, to my sensation, there's not much of a
difference between being 100% and 70% full. Yet, those 30% is another
massively tiring hour on the treadmill. I also cut back the same way on red
meat, processed food, MSG, and so on.

The great thing about incrementalism is that it's so tiny that I can barely
notice it, so, psychologically, I have the full control in managing my
behavioral change through it. Love and relations: I think the kind of love and
relations among people that will last is the one that is consistent. Many might
think that it's good enough if it doesn't decline over time. We humans tend to
want to seek more stories from newer relations, but that's because we think
that stories have to be new. They don't.

They can be something that is built up over time. We all have great stories to
tell, brag and regret, about our own personal development. It's the same.
Writing a blog: One day at a time.

            In Praise of Self-Improvement

title: In Praise of Self-Improvement
excerpt: AS I MENTIONED, I WANT the last 5 posts to be about the "self-improvement values" that I have held dearly. That said, I have not always been able to practice them. There had certainly been times when I let my immediate emotion, gut feeling,…
body:
AS I MENTIONED, I WANT the last 5 posts to be about the "self-improvement

values" that I have held dearly. That said, I have not always been able to
practice them. There had certainly been times when I let my immediate
emotion, gut feeling, belief, and heuristics took over my values, resulting in
my failures to perform what I have set out to. These values are different from
the "moral values" that I have outlined in my 12 rules.

In my last post, I wrote about my first self-improvement values --
incrementalism. Here're the other four. First, Quality (over quantity): There's
no other way to put it: It's more important to me to get one thing done right,
rather than get many things done half-right. We're living in the world
dominates not only by speed and scale, but also the madness of obsession
over quantity.

These things range from who earn more, to who have received more awards
and recognitions, to who have more possessions, such as luxury items,
houses, cars, etc. I grew up in a family that valued the quality of the work we
did -- the kind of work that made us proud of our contribution to our family,
company, institution and society -- and such quality was not always
represented by how much we earned or how much we're being recognized
publicly.

True, one would be lucky to be able to do massive amount of quality work.
My point is that if I have to choose between quality and quantity, I would go
for the former. Often, if not always, we don't have the luxury to choose both.
Once the quality is achieved, and there's a possible that it can be achieved
with multiplicity, then, certainly, why not.

Second, Process (over result): This has been the mantra with which I often
shout at myself whenever I begin to expect that I should get whatever I want
as a result of the work done well. Many thinkers, especially the Stoics, have
reminded us again and again that we suffer when we try to control things we
do not have any control over. We can control our value and opinion -- how
much work we want to put into the work by ways of own willpower.

The result, on the other hand, unless it's judged by you as well, is not in your
control. Of course, you may think that your work is good enough to get a
good result (in fact, as the psychologists Daniel Kanehman and Amos Tversky
have reminded us, we tend to overestimate our ability to plan and therefore
over-expect our ability to achieve), but even if that which is the case, you
must not undermine other factors that could shape the result.

So, as the Stoics suggest, we might as well ignore the anticipation for a
desirable result altogether, and focus solely on the process. I am personally
an "anti-consequentialist." Consequentialism preaches the idea that the
morality of an action is to be judged solely by its consequences. In the words
of Deng Xiaoping about whom I wrote in my Anti-Fragile Manifesto post, "It
doesn't matter whether a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice.

" This "Deng-ism," as some political scientists call it, came in timely and
handy at the time when Deng had to deal with problems from all fronts and
had lifted China out of its extreme poverty in the decades preceding his
re-emergence in the chaotic scene of Chinese politics as the supreme leader;
yet, there will be always be something rather unsettling lingering in many's
minds about that statement.

If a "black cat," is a malignant means, it should not justify any result even if
that result happens to be benign. Would you do anything to achieve your goal
including hurting others? My answer is a qualified no. In fact, I suggest that
you stay away from anyone who would do anything to excel, because for
someone like that there's virtually no guarantee that hurting you won't be on
that person's plan to benefit him/herself.

Historians, analysts, critics have argued that it's precisely the use of these
"black cat" methods to catch the economic mice that erodes the collective
sense of morality in the contemporary Chinese society the three decades
that follow, causing massive and irreversible social problems that may have
exceeded its initial benefits.

Third, Unconventional (over conventional): My favorite poem of all time by
Robert Frost begins like this: "Two roads diverged in a yellow wood, And
sorry I could not travel both." Like the poet, "I took the one less traveled by,
And that has made all the difference" but not for the same reason. It could be
the case that roads that are less travelled are dangerous, long and winding
and therefore inefficient as a travel route.

It could also be the case that it's "easier" to just take the road frequently
traveled because, that way, we do not have to think: We just follow. That's
precisely what conventionalism means -- the idea that what is "generally"
held by the mess is to be thought as acceptable at the expense of
individuality. I don't care much about my own individuality.

I am only interested in the truth, in giving the fair picture to what is not
"generally held by the mess as acceptable." As the clinical psychologist Dr
Jordan B Peterson remarks, "In order to think, you have to risk" -- your life,
damaging your own pre-existing belief, and offending other conventional
minds who are too lazy to put their brains to work. When I see a road less
travelled, I feel the urge to know more about it.

It's too easy to just do whatever most people like to do, which is another
classic fallacy (i.e., "social proof"). In fact, I enjoy using my brain to think

about the desertion of the road less travelled, and if I could not find any
sensible reasons that render it dangerous, I will take the less, instead of
frequently travelled road.

As the philosopher Immanuel Kant remarks, "enlightenment is mankind's
emergence from its self- imposed immaturity," something in me makes me
believe that taking the road less travelled is the path to that enlightenment. A
bonus: Taking the road less travelled makes life more exciting. Why do you
want to take the same road again and again? Fourth, Good enough (over
perfect): Perfectionism isn't a quality but a mental state that only exists in the
mind.

So, those who confuse what perfectionism are those who often suffer from
their inability to let go, move on. They're usually burdensome to others
expecting their timely delivery of a project, result, etc. I have met a number
of seemingly intelligent people who could not finish anything on time. And
their excuses?

You bet it is "Because I am a perfectionist." And these people always shake
their heads when I tell them, "Why don't you try to be just good enough
instead?" Whatever we do in life, we'll always be constrained by space and
time. These are the two domains of the phenomenal world in which we live.
Surely, if you have all the time in the world, you may be able to bring
anything and everything into its finest perfection.

But when do we ever have all the time in the world? That's never the nature
of any assignment. Space, too, plays in the same role in constraining our
ability to maneuver and deliver our products. In addition, precisely because
the space and time, what's "perfect" in one place and time might not be
perfect in the other. I personally suffered a lot during the writing phrase of
my doctoral dissertation.

Even after three years of data collection (which was unusually long for a
doctoral research) I still felt as though my work wasn't good enough, Not
because I was a perfectionist (spoiler alert: far from it) per se but because I

feared my advisor' disappointment if the work delivered to him was of the
less-than-perfect quality.

It was this advisor who guided me with his wise words, "Perfectionism is a
form of arrogance." After that, I shifted my focus from "producing a perfect
work that won't disappoint my advisor" to "producing a good enough work
that reflects the three years of quality data collection and its careful
analysis." You wouldn't believe how fast I could move with my writing that
had stalled for more than a year preceding that.

As I believe in quality over quantity, I took time to focus closely on my
collected data, analysis and writing. I wasn't able to deliver a magnum opus of
a dissertation that was 500-page-thick, but I was able to complete a good
enough work that represented some competence in my field of study. Of
course, my advisor did not go over the moon with my work, but he wasn't
dreadfully disappointed either.

For the philosopher Plato, "Forms" are being represented by objects in the
real world; hence there will never be perfect. My dissertation was that object
that represents the "ideal form" of that dissertation.

                 Problematizing Time

title: Problematizing Time
excerpt: ONCE UPON A TIME, A wise man I spoke to. "There's no problem; there's only time," said he. Little did I understand. Years passed, and soon I rephrased: A brush called time paints a canvas we call "space." Time intensifies the slate. "Space,…
body:
ONCE UPON A TIME, A wise man I spoke to. "There's no problem; there's only

time," said he. Little did I understand. Years passed, and soon I rephrased: A
brush called time paints a canvas we call "space." Time intensifies the slate.
"Space," and time: We need space to exist, to be, in a living paradigm. Time
prevents everything to happen all the once. "Space," and time are the brush
and canvas. In the realm we live, these two can't be apart. Otherwise, from
our mind we can never depart.

This canvas, however, is far from objective: Painted on it, the thing may not
make me feel as intensive, In a perspective. Sometimes, I allow time to paint
that very canvas, intensely. Which makes me I experience time,
problematically. The wise man was right, cleverly. Relative, time also is.

Einstein may or may not have said: "Waiting for a pretty girl for a minute, and
it seems like an hour." "Talking to the same girl for one hour, It seems like,
Just one minute." Time is relative, I soon realize: Pain lasts longer than
pleasure, both in temporally and in size. Ruled by time, the canvas, and
homogenized. One minute with pain feels like an eternity, While a year of
pleasure is simply negligible. How unfair? We cry over a sudden spill of milk.

But we don't appreciate the presence, This very moment we're still living.
What, then, should I do with time? In other words, All my problems, what
should I do with? All is equal thus far: Pleasure and pain, Are on par. A
problem is not just time, It needs time, To problematize. Give that problem
more time, It disintegrates, de-intensify. Enjoy both pain and pleasure. As
they come, And they go, Relatively speaking.:-) The End.

                What I Know for Sure

title: What I Know for Sure
excerpt: IT'S GETTING THERE. I AM 3 days away from the 100th day -- hooray. I can't wait. It would be wonderful to finally reach the milestone I set for myself over three years ago. I obviously overestimated my ability to be both consistent and pers…
body:
IT'S GETTING THERE. I AM 3 days away from the 100th day -- hooray. I can't

wait. It would be wonderful to finally reach the milestone I set for myself over
three years ago. I obviously overestimated my ability to be both consistent
and persistent. The 100th day was set to be finished around mid-2016, so I
will be about 300% tardy when, hopefully, the 100th day post was finally
written within the next few days.

The psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky call this a "planning
fallacy," of which I may be a prime example: I planned because I thought I
could do it, but I failed to do it. But if we may think about it from a different
angle, that's actually life. How many times things in life actually goes as
planned?

When I planned to write "a blog a day for one hundred consecutive day"
(hence, the name of the blog), I was not just being optimist about my ability
to pursue it, but I also deliberately took for granted the nature of life: That
shit happens. Metaphorically (luckily) shit is what we least or don't fully
expect (call it a black swan if you may). When shit happens, we are forced to
slow down, recalibrate, and re-position ourselves.

That's why things don't go as planned, and time is usually the first thing that
shit affects. In this post, I want to write about those shit and disruptions. In
other words, I want to write about what (I think) I absolutely know for sure
about life. These are, to me, categorical truths based on my experience living
in this world (and dealing with disruptions in life that result in the delay the
completion of this blog and fuels the planning fallacy.

Here you go: 12 things I know for sure. The fastest way to feel unpleasant is to
compare yourself (or your situation) to others (self-pity and ego-boost are no
good); Unless you let them, how others think about you cannot affect you
(they can even take your soul if you let them, oh, but don't you let them); In
doing things, ego is the enemy (so, kill it).

and obstacle is the way (so, embrace it); Being seen doing good it doesn't
make it "more good"; in fact, it feels much better to do it without anyone else
knowing but you (try it!); Unhappiness is a result of our failed attempt to
control that which we are not in control of (And, how many things in life we
are really in control of?

Stoics would say just your value and your opinion ); Those who enjoy judging
others do not like to be judged; so, they justify judging others as a form of
self-defense; When focusing in the presence, you cannot possibly worry
about anything (Go on, try it; don't think about the past or the future, you
won't be able to think about anything that can possibly worry you); Even
about things we think we know well, there's still almost a 100% chance that
our knowledge is not all complete (so let's assume that the person we are
talking to might know something we don't, and learn); Bad news appear more
often then good news in the media, and people don't use the internet to tell
the world about who they really are; so, don't mistake what you see on the
internet as the representation of the world.

We can actually become better versions of ourselves by reading books.
Whenever shit happens, turn yourself to some great books. Silence is less
harmful, less violence, and altogether better than gossip and nonsense prose.
The world is not perfect -- nothing is -- but it's still good.

     Why Reading News Isn't Good for
                  You

title: Why Reading News Isn't Good for You
excerpt: YES, I ONLY LEARNED ABOUT the Thai soccer team in the cave after they'd gotten out of the cave... Shame on me, right? Not so fast! I also only learn about a lot of things, not through reading news, but through words of mouth from family mem…
body:
YES, I ONLY LEARNED ABOUT the Thai soccer team in the cave after they'd

gotten out of the cave... Shame on me, right? Not so fast! I also only learn
about a lot of things, not through reading news, but through words of mouth
from family members, colleagues, friends, cab drivers, baristas and those
whom I casually converse with on the street. The news, if they're relevant to
me, will come to me regardless, I believe. I don't consume news. Period.

One time, one of my good friends even madly freaked out when I told him
about the fact that I haven't been reading news for almost three years, that I
don't have a Facebook account, and that I do not intentionally consume any
forms of news at all. "How do you even survive?" Well, truth be told, I have
survived -- and have been happy. First, most of the news, to me, are not
relevant to my personal life.

Second, knowing that news are made overly sensational to sell, I don't see
the point of consuming them. As a famous Zen monk once said (and I
paraphrase this from Eckhart Tolle's A New Earth ), "events are not
personalized, so there's no point in engaging in human drama" (especially
knowing that they're made sensationalized at the expense of your emotion), I

find even more reason to not stay away from intentionally consuming news.
Does this make sense to you?

[gallery ids="4858,4859,4860,4861" type="rectangular"] Research for this post
comes from these literatures. At first, I actually didn't think it made much
sense to me either -- until I found some key ideas from some of the key
thinkers about the dark side of news that resonated with my very own. Let's
start with that of Rob Dobelli. News reports do not represent the real world.
Our brains are wired to pay attention to visible, large, scandalous,
sensational, shocking, people-related, story-formatted, fast changing, loud,
graphic onslaughts of stimuli.

Our brains have limited attention to spend on things that are small, abstract,
ambivalent, complex, slow to develop and quiet, much less silent. News
organizations systematically exploit this bias. I agreed with this statement
but I honestly didn't truly understand what he meant at first. It's until I spoke
to a well-known journalist at one of the most respected newspapers (of
course I won't tell you which newspaper this was).

What's said to me was frank, vivid, and appealing: We know that every event
has at least two sides of the story; the problem for us -- who need to sell
news for living -- is that if we were to truly investigate all sides of such event,
you can bet that such event will be no longer newsworthy. You won't
understand us unless you're in the business of journalism.

That is, for this senior journalist to produce enough news for the said
newspaper to actually get himself paid a salary, he needed to turn a blind eye
on what he knew for sure would add depths, and more importantly the
notion of truth and balance, to the story. But not all truths are sensational --
usually they aren't. He's basically saying that "everything can go" if he can sell
the news. They're just facts that all of us can understand, empathize and even
sympathize.

So, those won't sell and therefore won't be included in the news. I am not
saying that all journalists operate this way, but there's some truth in it. Think

about it. What would you do if you're in his position. Again, Dobelli
summarizes the following points: As a result of news, we walk around with
the completely wrong risk map in our head. Terrorism is overrated. Chronic
stress is underrated. The collapse of Lehman (one of the world biggest
financial institutions in 2008) is overrated. Fiscal irresponsibility is
underrated.

Astronauts are overrated. Nurses are underrated. Britney Spears is overrated.
IPCC Reports are underrated. Airplane crashes are overrated. Resistance to
antibiotics is underrated. You get the point. All of the "overrated" events
above have the much less chance of happening statistically, but they're
"sensational." News direct us away from the "underrated" events that are
much more relevant to our lives -- how many times in this life you'll be
talking to a nurse than to an astronaut?

How many times do we see any news agencies' reporting the skillfulness of
an airplane pilot who shrewdly land a plane, and therefore saving hundreds
of innocent lives? Zero. A plane crash, on the other hand, is "overrated" and
repeatedly reported to the point that many of us could have a screwed view
of aviation industry. What good does that do? I wrote a while ago about how
our brain is prone to make a decision based on a mental shortcut (known as
"heuristics").

The path of this shortcut is built by the availability of the information that we
have about something. So, you'll tend to the think that the world is unsafe, if
the television that you've been constantly watching is showing a lot of news
about terrorism --- when we know that which is not the accurate portrayal
of the society, but, rather and sadly, what sells. The "availability bias," what
the psychologists would call this phenomenon, pave the way to our
misleading view of the society.

Think about how this could lead to a much more serious problems in
communication, in organizational management. Would you like to live in the
world in which everyone bases their judgement on the very little knowledge
that they have? So, why continue to be a victim of such thing?

In Enlightenment Now:The Case for Reason, Science, Humanism, and
Progress, the psychologist Steven Pinker shows us the data to support his
claim that: Whether or not the world really is getting worse, the nature of
news will interact with the nature of cognition to make us think that it is.
News is about things that happen, not things that don't happen.

We never see a journalist saying to the camera, "I'm reporting live from a
country where a war has not broken out"—or a city that has not been
bombed, or a school that has not been shot up.

As long as bad things have not vanished from the face of the earth, there will
always be enough incidents to fill the news, especially when billions of
smartphones turn most of the world's population into crime reporters and
war correspondents." Like Pinker, Dobelli himself has carefully studied this
phenomenon and his understanding was aligned with what I heard from the
senior editor of a respected newspapers to whom I spoke. He writes: "Some
journalists take time with their stories and try to think things through.

But like any profession, and probably more so than in any other profession,
journalism has its incompetent, unfair practitioners who don't have the time
– or the capacity – for deep analysis." Here is the truth of capitalistic
news-making: Often, they are paid by the piece, the line, or the word. You
might not be able to tell the difference between a polished professional and a
rushed, glib, paid-by-the-piece writer with an ax to grind. It all looks like
news. Scary, right?

So why are we continue to read whatever "all looks like news?" Are we that
innocuous, naive, or stupid? Or, it's because there's a certain belief that
whatever is in the news, at least, do have some truth in it? In his study,
Dobelli argues that " Fewer than ten percent of the news stories are original.
Fewer than one percent are truly investigative." Dobelli, like the psychologist
Steven Pinker, doesn't hate news completely. Neither do I actually.

He, in fact, argues for the "hard- core journalists digging into meaningful
stories. We need reporting that polices our society and uncovers the truth."

He claims, and I agree, that Watergate a good example, but truly this kind of
investigative journalism takes time, efforts, and a lot of balancing to see the
whole picture clearly so that the event can be reported with the fullest sense
of journalistic responsibility. I totally agree.

I think there're areas of the society that the authority doesn't want us to
know, so they buy out every effort that could bring them to life. We need the
Boston Globe-typed "Spotlight Team" to help uncover them, showing us what
the society is like. You might be aware of the fact that what these
investigative journalists are doing are much less of presenting a "dark side" of
the society and the world as such, but showing what lies beneath the
seemingly benign surface of events, things, and people.

Again, I do hope that this is the only kind of journalism that exists.
Unfortunately, tabloids, commercial newspapers, etc, are still the standard,
and our brain are wired by wanting to "hear stories" (read or listen to
Jonathan Gottschall's Storytelling Animal podcast) to the point that we find
them addictive. By the way, we haven't even begun to touch upon the topic of
"fake news" (which, by the way, is the new word has made its way into the
Oxford Dictionary this year) in this post.

In short, Dobelli argues that news (as they're presented to us 99% of the time
today) isn't useful because (and be ready): News misleads us systematically,
limits understanding, massively increase cognitive errors, makes us passive,
and kills creativity. There's another thing. Our brains have very limited
capacity to store data to be processed, so the more you know doesn't
necessarily mean the more you would be able to make use of what you know.

In fact, there is a very accurate psychological fact about this notion of the
brain that is best expressed, surprisingly, by one of the favorite fictional
heroes, Sherlock Holmes.

Here's his conversation with his buddy Dr Watson in A Study in Scarlet (and
this just show that the author Sir Arthur Conan Doyle knew about this fact all
along even before he constructed the character Holmes): I consider that a

man's brain originally is like a little empty attic, and you have to stock it with
such furniture as you choose.

A fool takes in all the lumber of every sort that he comes across, so that the
knowledge which might be useful to him gets crowded out, or at best is
jumbled up with a lot of other things, so that he has difficulty laying his hands
upon it. Now the skillful workman is very careful indeed as to what he takes
into his brain-attic.

He will have nothing but the tools which may help him in doing his work, but
of these he has a large assortment, and all in the most perfect order. It is a
mistake to think that that little room has elastic walls and can distend to any
extent. Depend upon it there comes a time when for every addition of
knowledge you forget something that you knew before. It is of the highest
importance, therefore, not to have useless facts elbowing out the useful ones.

So, there you have it: Why I don't read news and why I am still doing just fine
with my life and maintaining my livelihood. News, as Dobelli says, is like sugar
to the body. It's sweet and it plays with the part of our brain that makes us
feel addicted (for more on sugar, I recommend an episode of Freakonomics
Radio called There's A War On Sugar. Is It Justified? ). Our body doesn't need
sugar.

It doesn't need the kind of artificial neuro-stimulation that will end up giving
us weight gain, blood sugar problems and an increased risk of heart disease,
among other dangerous conditions. Yep, why don't you try putting in "the
benefits of sugar" on your Google Search -- you'll find nothing.

My good friend rebutted his argument that knowing some trivial facts
"helped to generate conversations, small talks, and positive gossips (by which
he meant a harmless gossip that bond people together." True, but is there
such thing as a "positive gossip?" Again, the words of Holmes (actually, of
Doyle's) ringers, and remember them, "It is of the highest importance,
therefore, not to have useless facts elbowing out the useful ones." So, let's
stray away from useless facts -- and most of time, as journalists love to

report, the useless not-even-facts, and return to great books, classics,
scientifically-proven research and ideas.

They aren't sugar, and they will certainly equip you with the knowledge that
you will need to make your life in this world more meaningful in a long run.

   The 44 Books that Change My Life

title: The 44 Books that Change My Life
excerpt: LET ME SAVOR THE MOMENT a bit. What moment? Come on, you know. It's the moment of reaching the "100th post milestone" that, by the way if you haven't already known from the name of this blog, I had set for myself at the onset of this post. …
body:
LET ME SAVOR THE MOMENT a bit. What moment? Come on, you know. It's the

moment of reaching the "100th post milestone" that, by the way if you haven't
already known from the name of this blog, I had set for myself at the onset of
this post. Shamelessly, and whatever-bad-habit-ly of mine, life as such has
obviously taken me more than 100 days to get to this point -- to be precise,
it's been about 1,300 days since I had set out to start this blog in 2015.

In other words, I am showing how a classic error that cognitive and social
psychologists would call a "planning fallacy" operates in reality (check out a
Freakonomics Radio episode for a crash course on the topic).

I have alluded to this fallacy a few times in my earlier posts; in short,
proposed by the two great psychologists (and my heroes, if you don't already
know) Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, is a phenomenon in which our
own predictions about how much time that will be needed to complete a
future task often, if not always, display too much of an optimism bias. What
this planning fallacy causes is the underestimation of the amount of time
needed to complete such task.

Come to think about it, I have never, in my life, finished any task on the day
that I thought I would. Ever. For This blog, I didn't think that it would be so

hard to write something every day -- just a post a day. How hard would that
be? But it's turned out to be very difficult to keep up with writing, even just a
blog a day, amidst other things in life of which I also have to take care.
There're other tasks and other deadlines.

There're times of tremendous emotional baggage that get in the way of
writing. And there are, of course, occasional laziness. There're physical
fatigue. And there are weddings to attend. How hard is it to write something
every day? Very hard, indeed: There're ad hoc work to take priority -- you
get the point. The lesson here is, whenever you're planing a task, make sure
that your actual deadline is at least a few weeks beyond your "optimistic
deadline" you'd like to unrealistically set for yourself.

O.k., enough about my inability to do things. Let's get to what I would like to
write about. For the past 5 years, I have been reading more than 300 books
(yep, I don't have much of a life). Reading is something that I utterly enjoy,
especially when I bike, drive, or do other mechanical things. I have to thank
my own discovery of e-books and audiobooks. It's been a hassle to carry
books with me wherever I go.

I travel abroad a lot, and the joy of reading physical books is usually blocked
by my unwillingness to carry them in my already-pretty-heavy bag. Books
are burdensome. So, e-books are great. I could read them on my phone,
laptop, tablet, etc. I could search, copy, cite, and so on, also, very easily. It's
difficult at first but after a while, as I've evolved evolutionarily, I have
embraced the technology. Now, it's hard for me to go back to physical books,
ever again.

I haven't bought any books, too, since 2013. Every day, also, I travel from
home to work and back; during which, apparently, there is a plenty of "lost
time" during my travel. This lost time is a traffic engineering term referring
to he time during which no vehicles are able to pass through an intersection
despite the traffic signal displaying a go signal. Same here, I could be reading
a book while driving but that wouldn't make it safe for me, would it?

What can you really do when you're driving, or on a metro or a bus? So, ever
since I've discovered audiobooks, I've begun to utilize those loss time and
turn them into learning time and productive time. On average, an audiobook
is about 8- hour-long, which I could finish listening to it in about 4 days --
say, I spend about 2 hours a day traveling to work; hence, I only need 4 days x
2 hours to finish a book. That's something I consider miraculous.

In essence, I have read more books in the past 5 year then in my entire life!
My former student and now a careful thinker colleague from Poland, Robert,
has suggested the topic for this post (thanks, Robert!) I'll put these books in a
few categories -- each category contains more than 4 books (as the more
doesn't mean the better -- it just means indecisiveness).

I also take into account my very own "peak-end rule heuristics" and "thinking
fallacy" or the idea that I tend to remember more of the books that I have
recently read rather than what I read a while ago (check out an episode of
NPR The Hidden Brain podcast for a sweet discussion with the psychologist
-- the great man himself). So, keeping that in mind, I'll try to come up with
the best possible list.

I have mentioned most of these books in my earlier posts in one form or the
other. See if you can spot them.

On how to think: [gallery ids="4861,4774,4875,4906" type="rectangular"] On
how to be a better social being: [gallery ids="4876,4888,4891,4878"
type="rectangular"] On how to see the world as it actually is: [gallery
ids="4860,4874,4893,4910" type="rectangular"] On spirituality (and how to
keep        calm      and    carry      on):         [gallery      ids="4881,4882,4883,4677"
type="rectangular"]         On    how     to         be   better     at   writing:    [gallery
ids="4884,4885,4886,4887" type="rectangular"] On moderate-to-extreme self
improvement: [gallery ids="4890,4889,4892,4923" type="rectangular"] On
something          surprisingly   awesome:           [gallery   ids="4894,4897,4907,4925"
type="rectangular"]         On    creativity:        [gallery   ids="4898,4899,4900,4901"
type="rectangular"] Classics that I revisited and utterly enjoyed: [gallery
ids="4902,4903,4904,4905" type="rectangular"] On history, biography, and

stories that matter: [gallery ids="4911,4912,4908,4877" type="rectangular"] My
personal    favorites   which     are    sort   of   uncategorizable:    [gallery
ids="4909,4896,4879,4918" type="rectangular"] Alright, there you have it.

These are the books that have, in some ways, changed my life. I have just
realized that these are only 44 out of some 300 books that I have read (and
listen, as in audiobooks) over the past 5 years. The rest are also great, but
these are the creme de la creme. I perhaps still need to look for more books
to read and listen too -- it's so addictive. If someone asks me how I'd define
myself, I probably wouldn't say that I'm an architect or an anthropologist.

I'd proudly call myself a "reader."

 How to Deal with Unpleasant Events

title: How to Deal with Unpleasant Events
excerpt: A BEAUTIFUL GIRL IN THE village was pregnant. Her angry parents demanded to know who was the father. At first resistant to confess, the anxious and embarrassed girl finally pointed to Hakuin Ekaku, the Zen master whom everyone previously re…
body:
A BEAUTIFUL GIRL IN THE village was pregnant. Her angry parents demanded

to know who was the father. At first resistant to confess, the anxious and
embarrassed girl finally pointed to Hakuin Ekaku, the Zen master whom
everyone previously revered for living such a pure life. When the outraged
parents confronted Hakuin with their daughter's accusation, he simply
replied "Is that so?" When the child was born, the parents brought it to the
Hakuin, who now was viewed as a pariah by the whole village.

They demanded that he take care of the child since it was his responsibility.
"Is that so?" Hakuin said calmly as he accepted the child. For many months he
took very good care of the child until the daughter could no longer withstand
the lie she had told. She confessed that the real father was a young man in
the village whom she had tried to protect. The parents immediately went to
Hakuin to see if he would return the baby. With profuse apologies they
explained what had happened.

"Is that so?" Hakuin said as he handed them the child. I have copied this story
from John Suler's Zen Stories to Tell Your Neighbors, but I first read from it
in Eckhart Tolle's A New Earth: Awakening to Your Life's Purpose. In Tolle's
words, the Zen Master Hakuin simply did not want to "participate in human
drama." As Tolle describes: To the Zen Master Hakuin, there is only this

moment -- and this moment is as it is. Events are not personalized. He is
nobody's victim.

He is so completely at one with what happens that what happens has no
