"City in Nature" Pavilion represents an effort to create a net-zero building
architecture packed with plants and fans powered by clean energy. The most

successful pavilions, however, are those whose architectural designs fit the
exhibition and vice versa, rather than those with the most visually
spectacular architecture.

The exterior of the UK Pavilion, designed by Es Devlin Studio, is among the
most photographed pavilions; yet, the exhibitions it houses leave little
impression, because the building's focus is entirely on its facade, leaving
everything else comparatively sterile and deserted. Although interesting
architecture has always been one of the Expos' centerpieces, most attendees
are not architects and are thus more interested in other features such as
exhibitions, events, and performances.

            A Return to This Blog and
               Simulation Theory

title: A Return to This Blog and Simulation Theory
excerpt: SO I HAVEN'T DONE MUCH with this blog since 2019. I had to start a new job. It required I spend most of my time learning new things. So I didn't write much. Especially not reflective stuff. Like I used throughout this blog. Now we're in the…
body:
SO I HAVEN'T DONE MUCH with this blog since 2019. I had to start a new job. It

required I spend most of my time learning new things. So I didn't write much.
Especially not reflective stuff. Like I used throughout this blog. Now we're in
the age of AI. I'm using my voice. Also my semi touch-typing skill. To keep
writing this blog. I think I haven't fulfilled its purpose. Which was to help me
find a meaning of life. I know, I know. Sounds ambitious.

But think about it. Writing rearranges your thoughts. Makes them clear.
Trust me, clear thoughts mean better decisions. Better than when your
mind's cluttered. With all kinds of unprioritized thoughts. So I'm restarting
this blog. I hope I can write every day. It's good to reflect. Now ten years
passed. I'm leaving my midlife. Usually, you hear midlife, then crisis. I'm
dealing with that a bit. You cross halfway of your longevity. Normal people
realize they want to look back. See how much they walked.

How much they achieved, if any. I got two missions for rewriting this blog.
One, I want to recall memories. Crystal clear details. In the past, I wrote
everything. I got a five-year diary. Each page divides into five sections. I fill
one every day. Five years pass, all boxes are filled. With what happened that

day. I find it therapeutic. I get chills recalling memories. Like when I write
about someone I'm thankful for. I feel chills in my spine. It's nice to recall
grateful moments.

When things aren't right, I write. Even in that small box. A few lines. About
what happened. It makes me see the world different. I recognize the fault's
mine too. As much as theirs. In any wrongdoing. The other reason for
restarting? Capture my old writing sensibility. I used to be good. Made
references. Created metaphors. Came up with mnemonics no one could
touch. That faded over time. I didn't exercise it. During my dissertation, I
described everything. My reader could smell the scene. I was architectural.
Built a foundation.

Constructed    a      framework   of   thoughts.   Strong   internal    framing.
Counterarguments didn't stand. No flaws found. Then I slapped on the
façade. Pristine words people wanted to hear. Added windows and doors. Let
people enter my house. Experience it themselves. Come out feeling they
learned something. I painted comprehensive pictures. For those who weren't
there. Not just scenery. The smell. The feeling. The sixth sense. Something
beyond words. I used metaphors to project thoughts. I wanna recapture that
skill. That sensation. Something strong drove me to write like that.
Compelling.

Beyond the rigid domain of professional design. Obsessed with design, not
description. I must come clean. I didn't write here for years. I sought refuge
at Medium.com. It was well-formatted. Had chances to make money. If
people read more. I thought my experiences could help. Make folks feel
better. Or worse, who knows. I could earn from it. I got stories others don't.
Wrote a few pages there. Now I'm back to this blog. Where it started. It's
freedom. Not many read this. Unlike Medium. I can be free. Adamant.

Honest. I want people to feel what I feel. Maybe it resonates. Helps them see
their situation different. Like Daniel Kahneman said, nothing's as important
as it feels in that moment. He passed last year. Allegedly by his own will. Sad
for a decision-making expert. Helped lots of people. Him and Amos Tversky. I

like Kahneman. He knew how people mess up decisions. Got a Nobel for it.
Another story for later. Today. I wanna talk simulation. I've been obsessed a
long time.

Couldn't understand if we're in a real world. Or simulation. Goes back to
when I was young. Grew up in Bangkok. I'd lie on my wooden bed. Look at the
ceiling. Imagine small guys under my bed. Cities down there. Like our world.
But smaller. Their comic books? Stories about me. I was their interest. Only
subject. They lived by my clock. Wanted my news. Read it for fun. To learn.
Help their lives. I read tons of comics. Fantasy ones. Got me thinking.

If I can imagine being king of a kingdom nobody cares about, maybe I'm an
actor in one. Someone created. Nobody cares. Elon Musk said stuff about
this. If we look at computer speed today, it's wild. Moore's Law—transistors
double every two years. We're growing exponential. Tech's moving fast. Huge
investments. Especially in the US. Center of digital stuff. Soon, video games'll
be indistinguishable from reality. Maybe we're there. We're in a simulation
now. I thought this after The Matrix. Watched all three. Loved the third.
Unveiled mysteries. The Architect.

Neo. Free will questions. Got me deep when I taught philosophy in Shanghai.
Ten years ago. Starting this blog. I could dig deep. Rabbit hole's endless. Still
better than living for primitive stuff. Like sex. Food. Entertainment. Here's my
theory. I believe we're in a simulation. Computers develop too fast. We're
past real-life simulation already. That's nice, actually. If it's a simulation, we
don't gotta care much. If we care, it's better knowing it ends. Something good
comes next. Takes away thoughts of suicide. Or crimes. Why go crazy?

It's just a simulation. I got three hypotheses about how we got here. First one.
Simulation's a test. We don't know where it leads. What kind it is. Like a
SWAT movie. Fake experience to train. See if you crack. I think it's S.W.A.T.
with Colin Farrell. The protagonist's in a fake situation. I won't know till the
end. Walls collapse. He cracked. Lost the task. We're like that. Memories on
hold. Live in the simulation. No past. If you crack, wanna leave? You fail.
Could be a good theory.

Like pilot training. Simulate a crash. See if they save the plane. We don't
know what's at the end. Maybe it's good. You pass if you don't break. All we
gotta do? Do not take life too seriously. Get through calm. Second theory.
Simulation's punishment. We're here millions of times. I've typed this post a
million times. Family, love, breakups. Same girl. Same spot. Nothing changes.
Is Diet Coke on my table? Always there. In every simulation, I act the same.
Only way out? Realize it's a loop. Not easy.

Not hard. Don't put the Coke there. Try new. Then you're free. Aligns with
Buddhism. We give meaning to meaningless. That's why we suffer. Stop it,
you're free. I don't like repeating pain. Hope I'm close to seeing the loop.
Getting out. Third theory. Entertainment. We're in a world where lifelike
simulation's normal. AI works for us. We get everything free. No work.
Governments give universal basic income. People use it to play. Not police
them. Money keeps folks in line. Less harm than when they're poor. Hopeless.

Poor folks do bad stuff. Fentanyl. Crime. With cash, we enter simulations.
Someone bored picks my life. Lives it a bit. Wakes up. Tries another. Forever.
We cured cancer. Don't die. But endless life? Boring. Some want death. Not
an option. Utopian. Dystopian. Long life, no meaning? That's hell. I believe
these theories. Don't know which is true. Can't all be. I like the first. Test's
optimistic. Get out, find something real. Second's doom. Hard to escape
loops. Third's bad. Humans want no death. Then get bored. Hypocritical.

Why build civilization if we're just bored? I'm different now. Ten years ago, I
started this blog. Fresh. Now I have experience. Don't trust myself. Crack
under pressure. Don't trust others. They want good times. Not trust.
Writing's my thing. One line a day. Find meaning. Never a day without a line.
Citations: Kahneman's work: Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect
Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk. Econometrica, 47(2), 263– 291.
Moore's Law: Moore, G. E. (1965). Cramming More Components onto
Integrated Circuits. Electronics, 38(8).

Musk's simulation theory: Musk, E. (2016). Interview at Code Conference. Vox
Media.

                    Daniel Kahneman

title: Daniel Kahneman
excerpt: PEOPLE KEEP ASKING ME. RECOMMEND a book. I ask how many I can pick. They say just one. I always go for Thinking, Fast and Slow. By Daniel Kahneman. Great psychologist. Changed my life. I didn't read it first. I listened to the audio. I got …
body:
PEOPLE KEEP ASKING ME. RECOMMEND a book. I ask how many I can pick. They

say just one. I always go for Thinking, Fast and Slow. By Daniel Kahneman.
Great psychologist. Changed my life. I didn't read it first. I listened to the
audio. I got hooked after another book. It's called The Undoing Project.
Michael Lewis wrote it. It's a biography of two psychologists. Kahneman and
Amos Tversky teamed up for years. They changed how we understand
decisions: "We think we get stuff. We don't.

Even when we think we do." That's groundbreaking. It started behavioral
economics. Also social psychology stuff. Even positive psychology. Source:
https://wizdomapp.com/books-like-thinking-fast- and-slow/ It got me
thinking hard. I was in academia back then. I was job hunting. I wanted
psychology in my work since I found it to be so cool (even wanted a
psychologist girlfriend at one point). I studied anthropology. I regretted not
taking psych classes. They were so close. Just a few floors up. William James
Hall at Harvard. That book pulled me in.

I wanted to know this Princeton Professor Daniel Kahneman. He was alive
then. Stanford Professor Amos Tversky passed years ago. He had an illness.
Kahneman lasted till March last year. He chose assisted suicide. He left at 90.
He did it before physical decline. Before cognitive decline. That hit him. It's

strange for a top psychologist. He won the Nobel Prize in Economics. That
was in 2002. He's an expert in decision- making. Yet he cut his life short. He
didn't face it all.

I can't believe his family was okay with it.

Something feels off. I'm Buddhist. I believe life's a journey. If you take
shortcuts like suicide, you start over. You never reach the end. I'm not sure if
that's pure Buddhism. It might be my simulation theory. I'm obsessed with
that idea. I'll talk about that next section. First, I wanna focus on Kahneman. I
picked up Thinking, Fast and Slow after The Undoing Project. It's a great
book. It feels conversational. It's like Uncle Danny's talking to you. He
explains with experiments. Some use people. Some use objects.

He shows how we think: He breaks thinking into two systems. System 1 is hot.
We use our gut. It happens when we're anxious. Or when we're excited.
We're not true to our thinking self. System 1 isn't all bad. Something happens
fast? You react. Your body takes over. Your brain avoids trouble. System 1
kicks in. You escape. System 2 is logical. It's cooler. You take your time. You
think about factors. You think about consequences. You think about
processes. You avoid problems. You get results quick.

System 2 folks are like that Star Trek guy (Spalk? I wouldn't know since I am a
Star Wars guy). He's always logical. The rest of the crew? Like the captain. He
acts on his gut. He acts on his heart. He's a System 1 guy. Source: Reed and
Proctor, Collegiality, dealing with bullying and acknowledging unconscious
bias. https://www.slideserve.com/Pat_Xavi/dignity-at-work The book is
thick. It's not thin. It takes a week to finish. But it's fun to read. I listened first.
Then I got the paperback.

The second time, I learned more. We're so flawed. We're bad at decisions. It
became my favorite book. I like other books too. Ryan Holiday's trilogies on
living good. Michael Lewis's books on money. Also cryptocurrency. Malcolm
Gladwell's stuff. I loved his first 10 years. He wrote non-fiction. Later, he got
wack. His messages turned cloudy. Too much virtue signaling. A patronizing

tone. People read him less now. Danny taught us humans something. Even
logical stuff isn't easy. Like self-preservation. Outside stimuli mess us up. The
past clouds our judgment.

The present makes us nervous. We can't decide easy. We become less logical.
We're less reasonable. When we engage in a process. We didn't think about
that before. Then Kahneman came. He shaped psychology. He made it about
thinking. That's amazing. I gotta set the scene. Before Kahneman, psychology
was scientific. You'd see machines in offices. They collected data. They
formed side effect theories. Like B.F. Skinner did. Behavioralism. It shaped
conditioning. People were triggered to act. They didn't know. Intuition got
planted in their mind. Before they even knew.

Research was experimental. It was mathematical. It used stats a lot. Pigeons
being observed and tested in a B.F. Skinner's laboratory at Harvard. Source:
https://www.sciencephoto.com/media/706878/view Psychology was a
hard science. Then Kahneman and Tversky came along. They showed the
world. You don't need all that equipment. Psychology is about studying
people. People walking around us. It's a systematic understanding. Of their
psych. Machines can't always catch it. You use experiments. But they can be
simple. Or casual. You ask people. Same situation. Same condition. They give
different answers.

You find reasons. Behind their differences. In how they think. Daniel came.
Amos came. They changed the discipline. Psychology got grounded. It got
relatable. It's not too far from psychiatry. People think psychologists
diagnose. In hospital rooms. Or they're psychotic. They're into psychology. I
don't know. My understanding of psychology was different. Before these two
guys. Their contribution was amazing. They got us thinking. About the
spectrum of human indecision. In situations needing good decisions. We
overestimate risk. We underestimate rewards. From a bet or something. They
uncovered biases.

Problems with anchoring. We're mentally anchored. We walk into losing
situations. We lack priming psychology. They showed us. With vivid

examples. Vivid experiments. It made me believe. Psychology teaches us.
Who we are. How we are. How we get where we wanna be. If life has meaning
at all. I'll move quick. You know enough. I love his work. It helped me think.
About myself. System 1 crashes with System 2. I want System 2 in public.
Inside, I'm System 1. It's my bad temper. I'm trying to control it.

I'm not great at it. Decisions come from info I get. Beforehand. Those are
biases. It's interesting. The number of biases. The anchoring stuff. We see
things. We hear things. We feel things. Before walking into a room. Without
that noise, I'd decide different. That's big for me. Now, the real topic.
Kahneman's assisted suicide. I don't think he should've done that. For many
reasons. He could still talk. He could be stimulating. Like he always was. He
was useful to humanity. It's not fair for humanity. To control him.

Live or die. To be or not to be. That's Hamlet stuff. But still. Something might
happen tomorrow. A medical breakthrough. It could cure his problem. Maybe
3D printing kidneys. If that's his issue. In a few years, we might make his
brain. It'd be more helpful. To society. We could ask him stuff. Like a mini
ChatGPT. All wisdom. A System 2 guy. He didn't leave options. For those
things. He traveled to Switzerland. First, he went to France. With family.
Visited his birthplaces. Old spots. To remember.

Before he died. Then Switzerland. For the suicide. Humanity shouldn't ask
him. To stay forever. But we gave him a lot. He owed a lot. How he became
him.

Contributions he could've had. If he believed in humanity. We'd fix what he
had. It'd be easy. He could've conquered decay. Physical decay. It makes us
who we are. He could've lived long. Studied more. Learned more. Solved
more problems. Done cutting-edge research. Led us better. The world's
divided now. Too many poles. People think too much. There's rising conflicts.
Wars. Declining birth rates. They're related. He should've stayed. I think it's
selfish of him. People around him thought he was senile. That he could
decide for himself. That's ridiculous.

Really ridiculous. To cue a guy. Just cause he seemed capable. He lacked
belief. In human goodness. We should've tried. Got him to a point. Where we
could help. He contributed so much. He could live longer. Help more. Be one
of us. It's too early. I learned lessons. First lesson. Your life is an accumulation
of experience. Go out there. Find more. Born in France. Grew up in
Jerusalem. Professor in the US. He embraced multiculturalism. Few people
have that. It shaped his personality. His character. Made him empathetic.

Calm in turmoil. He saw perspectives ahead. From exposure. Different
thinking. Different beliefs. Different processes. These are qualities of a great
mind. (I'm like him. I lived in Bangkok. Shanghai. Boston. I got those values.
That empathy. It makes me good at what I do. I wanna keep that. I learned
from this. It's a sample size of one. But it's a good example. Daniel himself).
Second lesson. We're who we tell ourselves to be. Social psychology says
that. It was born under these two. It's about individuals. In society.

Society dictates behavior. Wanna act pure? Raw? Unedited? In a good way.
Hide primitive desires. That's another topic. Sigmund Freud. Not Kahneman.
To be you? Act willingly. Think who you are. Believe it first. Believing in your
ability. To be you. In that moment. It confirms you're good. Lets you decide
good. I like that. It works. It makes me confident. Sometimes I force it. It's the
only way. When things get rough. Third lesson. You never know what's gonna
happen. The best decision- maker. On Earth. Commits suicide.

That's nonsense. It doesn't make sense. It taught me. You never fully
understand. Even if you think you do. Steve Jobs said it. Stay hungry. Stay
foolish. That's true. Stop being hungry? Once food's served? You can't
exercise. You can't go out. Be excited. Find new things. You go dormant.
You're useless. People wanna exercise. Be different. Be unexpected. Not like
this. Not Kahneman's choice. I can't believe he did that. I'll try my life. To
reconcile with it. That's the point. I hope you learned. I learned about me.

We're our problems. We think too much. About the moment. We believe it's
everything. Time makes it less hard. Unless it gets harder. Maybe leaving's
right. But that point won't come soon. If it does? You survive? You get a

bonus. You supply more later. Citations: Kahneman's work: Kahneman, D., &
Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk.
Econometrica, 47(2), 263–291. The Undoing Project: Lewis, M. (2016). The
Undoing Project: A Friendship That Changed Our Minds. W.W. Norton &
Company. Kahneman's Nobel Prize: The Nobel Prize.

(2002). Daniel Kahneman – Facts.

 Free Rides to Harvard, MIT, Oxford:
  How I Never Paid a Penny for My
              Education

title: Free Rides to Harvard, MIT, Oxford: How I Never Paid a Penny for My Education
excerpt: PEOPLE LOOK AT MY RESUME — MIT, Oxford, Harvard — and they ask, "How the hell did you pull that off, Non?" They expect me to say I had rich parents, a fat bank account, or that I was some child prodigy from Thailand who got a golden ticket …
body:
PEOPLE LOOK AT MY RESUME — MIT, Oxford, Harvard — and they ask, "How

the hell did you pull that off, Non?" They expect me to say I had rich parents,
a fat bank account, or that I was some child prodigy from Thailand who got a
golden ticket from the government. Nope. None of that. My family wasn't
rich. I wasn't born with a silver spoon or a brain that could solve quantum
equations at age ten.

I was just a regular kid, playing basketball, skipping classes, and dreaming big
in a small corner of Bangkok. So how did I end up at some of the world's top
schools without paying a single penny? Buckle up, because it's a story of grit,
rejections, and a whole lot of library hours. Back in the early 2000s, Thailand
wasn't the shiny metropolis it is now in 2025.

Bangkok was a chaotic, backwater town — no mass transit, traffic jams
everywhere, and if you wanted cool gadgets, you had to fly to Singapore or
Hong Kong. I grew up in rural Thailand, went to local middle and high
schools, and barely studied because I was too busy being a "lame kid" who
thought basketball was life. When I got into a normal college for architecture,

it wasn't exactly a victory parade. But something shifted when I hit 22, fresh
out of college. I wanted more.

I wanted to see the world, learn from the best, and not just be another cog in
Thailand's system. Here's the thing: I didn't have money, and I wasn't about
to take out loans or beg my parents for cash. I also didn't want a government
scholarship. Sure, they'd pay for your PhD, but then you're chained to a job
back home for twice as long as you studied — or you pay back triple the cost.
I saw friends get burned by that.

One buddy, whom I knew through a physics camp years back, a quantum
physics PhD from Harvard, came back and taught Physics 101—Newtonian
stuff any undergrad could handle. What a waste! His cutting-edge lab days
were over because Thailand didn't need quantum theory. I wasn't about to let
that happen to me. So, I got scrappy. The internet was crap back then, so I'd
visit university websites, request brochures by snail mail, and pore over them
like they were treasure maps.

I dreamed of studying abroad, but every path seemed blocked by money or
my mediocre high school grades. Then I realized something: my college years
had given me an edge. I'd spent five years in the library, not partying like my
architecture classmates. I wasn't the smartest, but I could outwork anyone.
My English was decent—not great, but enough to give me a head start.

And because I graduated top of my class (not hard in a third-tier college in a
developing country that I went to), I didn't pay a baht for my bachelor's
degree -- even though it would still be quite cheap, like US$300/semester.
After graduating, I started teaching at a Thai university, but I kept my eyes on
the horizon. I didn't want to be a professor living a cozy life in Bangkok. I
wanted to learn from places where free speech and bold ideas drove
innovation.

So, I hit the ground running — attending study-abroad fairs, researching
scholarships, applying to every grant I could find. Rejection letters piled up
like junk mail. England, Netherlands—some schools accepted me, but they

wanted tuition I couldn't afford. Loans? No way. With my measly teaching
salary, I'd be drowning in debt. But I kept going, like a fisherman fixing his
nets when the sea's too rough. I studied harder, improved my English, and
prepped for the GRE like my life depended on it. Rejections didn't faze me.

I'd laugh at them, toss them aside, and keep applying. By the 20th letter, I was
numb to failure. Then, one day, a package arrived: the Fulbright Scholarship.
The holy grail of grants—fully funded, two years in the U.S., no strings
attached. I'd applied the year before and bombed, but this time, I nailed the
interview. I walked in, criticized Thai politics, and laid it all bare. They saw me
for who I was: a kid from nowhere with a fire to learn. Fulbright changed
everything.

Suddenly, I had the funds, but I needed a school. I applied to a few, and MIT
said yes. MIT! They covered half my tuition, Fulbright and the Asian Cultural
Council handled the rest, and I even freed up some grant money for other
students. Fun fact: I was a Fulbrighter the same year as Rishi Sunak, who later
became UK prime minister. We probably crossed paths at some fancy
Fulbright event, but I didn't notice him —he was just another shy grad
student then.

MIT Graduation in 2008: Photo Credit: Reilly Rabitaille MIT was just the start.
I wanted more—a master's, a PhD. But the game got harder. Once you're in
the U.S., you're no longer the "underprivileged kid from a developing
country." My Thai accent was thick, my English writing was a mess, and GREs
and TOEFLs were brutal. Rejections poured in again—Stanford, MIT (yep,
they rejected me for a second degree). But I kept at it, fishing for grants like
my life depended on it.

After maybe 50 rejections, I landed the Harvard-Yenching Institute
Scholarship. It was a lifeline for Asian Studies research, and because I was a
faculty member in Thailand with solid recommendations, I squeaked
through. The catch? None actually. Harvard-Yenching let me start at Oxford
for a Master of Philosophy in Modern Chinese Studies. Two years later, I
applied to Harvard's Anthropology PhD program—one of the toughest, with a

1% acceptance rate. Stanford rejected me. MIT rejected me. But Harvard said
yes.

I'd done it again—no debt, no rich parents, just a kid who wouldn't quit.
Looking back, I realize my journey wasn't about being rich or a genius. It was
about finding the right opportunities and outlasting the rejections. In 2025,
with information at our fingertips, it's easier to track down scholarships, but
the mindset hasn't changed. You've got to dig, persist, and believe you're
worth it. Three Key Takeaways Research Like Your Future Depends on It I
didn't know scholarships like Fulbright or Harvard-Yenching existed until I
started digging.

Back then, I scoured university websites and begged for brochures. Today,
you've got Google—use it. Look for keywords like "merit- based scholarships"
or "grants for developing countries." There's money out there for people like
you, but you have to find it. The more you learn, the more doors open.
Embrace Rejection as Part of the Game I got rejected so many times—17
before Fulbright, 50 before Harvard-Yenching. Each "no" was a punch, but I
laughed it off and kept going. Rejections aren't personal; they're just noise.

If you let them stop you, you'll never find the "yes" waiting at the end. Keep
fixing your net, and the tide will turn. Teach Yourself to Win I wasn't born
smart. High school me was a slacker. But college me spent thousands of
hours in the library, reading, learning, and building skills. I taught myself
English, prepped for exams, and outworked everyone. You don't need a fancy
school to start—you just need a fire to learn and the discipline to follow
through.

In the end, I never paid a penny because I believed I could find a way. If a
regular kid from rural Thailand can do it, so can you. Stop thinking you need
money or a genius IQ. Start researching, keep pushing, and bet on yourself.
The world's waiting. This post is part of my 2025 revisit of my old blog, where
I'm digging up the stories that shaped me. Stay tuned for more.

      Finding Flow: Unlocking Joy and
            Efficiency in Work

title: Finding Flow: Unlocking Joy and Efficiency in Work
excerpt: PEOPLE OFTEN ASK ME, "NON, how do you get so much done? How are you so effective at work?" I laugh because, first of all, I don't always get stuff done. I often find myself in chaotic moments especially a day before any deadlines. However, …
body:
PEOPLE OFTEN ASK ME, "NON, how do you get so much done? How are you so

effective at work?" I laugh because, first of all, I don't always get stuff done. I
often find myself in chaotic moments especially a day before any deadlines.
However, there's some truth in the above question. I'm not some hyper-
focused genius or a productivity guru with a secret formula. Truth is, I'm just
a guy from Thailand who's figured out one thing: when I'm in flow, I can do
less but achieve more.

Flow is that sweet spot where time slips away, work feels effortless, and
you're fully alive in the moment. It's not just for laptop warriors like me—it's
for athletes, musicians, gamers, public speakers, anyone who's ever felt "in
the zone." Let me share how I stumbled into flow, why it's a game-changer,
and how you can find it too. What Is Flow? The psychologist Mihaly
Csikszentmihalyi put a name to this state in his groundbreaking work on
positive psychology.

Flow happens when you're so absorbed in a task that everything else fades
away—your worries, your to-do list, even the ticking clock. It's not about
working harder; it's about working smarter, where the challenge matches

your skills, and you're fully engaged. Csikszentmihalyi showed that flow isn't
just productive—it's joyful. It's why a guitarist loses herself in a riff, or a
runner feels invincible mid-stride. For me, flow is the key to meaningful
work, and I've chased it through writing, biking, and even my anthropological
fieldwork.

Source: https://www.siobhaindanaher.com/is-your-life-in-flow/ Flow in
My Life I first noticed flow years ago, biking along Shanghai's Huangpu River
on my Brompton foldable bicycle. Pedaling fast, wind in my face, I wasn't
thinking about my dissertation or career woes. I was just there, dreaming up
wild ideas— like alternate universes or new political systems—while listening
to Howard Zinn's A People's History of the United States through my
earbuds. Those rides were pure flow: my body moved, my mind soared, and
ideas clicked into place.

I'd come home buzzing, ready to pour those thoughts into my writing.
Writing itself is another flow trigger. When I'm drafting a blog post or a
chapter, I sink into a rhythm where words spill out naturally. It's like what I
wrote back in 2015: there's a gap between the spark of an idea and when my
fingers start typing, and in that gap, flow happens. I think more clearly,
connect dots I didn't see before. But it's not always easy to get there.

Early in my PhD, I'd hit writer's block, staring at a blank page, feeling like an
imposter. That's when I learned flow isn't something you force—it's
something you invite. Fieldwork taught me flow too. In Shanghai, sitting by
the river with my notebook, I'd observe people, jotting down their stories.
When I was truly present—not worrying about my next deadline or whether I
was a "real" anthropologist—I'd lose myself in their world. Those moments
weren't just productive; they were alive with meaning, like I was part of
something bigger.

Why Flow Matters In 2025, work can feel like a grind—endless emails, Zoom
calls, and deadlines. But flow flips that. It's not about clocking hours or
burning out; it's about doing work that matters with less effort. When I'm in
flow, I'm not dragging myself through tasks. I'm solving problems creatively,

writing with clarity, or leading smart city projects with energy.

Flow makes me better at what I do, and it feels damn good—like I'm living in
what the philosopher Leibniz would call "the best of all possible worlds." Flow
isn't just for me. It's universal. A chef perfecting a dish, a coder debugging a
program, a teacher captivating a classroom—they're all in flow when they're
fully immersed. Csikszentmihalyi's research backs this: flow boosts
performance and happiness because it aligns your focus with your purpose.
It's a stepping stone to expertise, too.

The more you enter flow, the more you recognize patterns—how problems
arise, how they're solved—and that's when you start mastering your craft.
How I Get into Flow I'm not a Pomodoro guy, setting timers to force
productivity. My approach is simpler: I set the stage for flow and let it find
me. Here's what works: Create the Right Vibe: I love coffee, so I play café
ambient noise on YouTube—clinking cups, hissing espresso machines. It's
like being in a bustling coffee shop, and it tricks my brain into focus mode.

Research suggests ambient sounds, especially with steady rhythms, can
enhance concentration by calming the mind. For me, it's like a warm hug that
says, "Get to work." Start with Low-Hanging Fruit: If I'm stuck, I pick a task I
know I can nail—a quick email, a simple edit. It's like warming up before a
bike ride. Completing something small builds momentum, and soon I'm
tackling bigger challenges without overthinking. This mirrors what I did in
fieldwork: start with easy observations, then dive into deeper stories.

Eliminate Distractions: I silence my phone and keep it out of sight. One call
can yank me out of flow, and it takes ages to get back. I also use a standing
desk—it keeps me alert and moving. Even short breaks, like walking to the
bathroom, help. That brief distance lets my thoughts settle, and I return
ready to dive in again. These aren't fancy hacks. They're small, intentional
choices that create space for flow.

Whether I'm writing a report or sketching ideas for a smart city project,
these techniques help me hit that sweet spot where work feels like play. Flow

and Expertise Flow isn't just about feeling good—it's how you grow. When I
was biking or writing, I wasn't just enjoying the moment; I was learning. Each
flow state helped me see patterns—how to structure a chapter, how to read a
city's rhythm, how to solve a problem. With AI tools in 2025, this is even
truer.

Once I'm in flow, I can spot repetitive tasks and automate them, freeing up
time for creative work. Flow turns you into an expert by sharpening your
instincts and making every effort count. Three Key Takeaways Invite Flow,
Don't       Force   It   Flow   isn't   about    grinding   harder.   Set   up    your
environment—music, a clear desk, no distractions—and start with something
easy to build momentum. It's like pedaling gently before a sprint. Let flow
come naturally, and you'll do more with less effort. Make Flow Your Standard
Don't work just to check boxes.

Aim to work in flow, where you're fully engaged and the task feels right. It's
not always possible, but prioritizing flow states—like I do when writing or
biking—makes your work more effective and joyful. Use Flow to Grow Each
time you enter flow, you're not just getting stuff done; you're honing your
craft. Notice the patterns, learn from them, and apply them. Whether you're
coding, teaching, or designing, flow is your path to expertise.

Final Thoughts Flow isn't a magic bullet, but it's the closest thing I've found to
making work feel like a gift. It's how I went from a kid in rural Thailand to
leading digital economy initiatives, writing blogs that resonate, and chasing
big ideas without burning out. In 2025, with all the noise of modern life, flow
is my anchor. It's not about being the smartest or the most disciplined—it's
about finding that place where you're fully you, doing work that matters, and
loving every second of it. Try it.

Set the stage, dive in, and see where flow takes you. This post is part of my
2025 revisit of my blog, where I'm sharing new stories built on old lessons.
More to come.

  Confidence Is Built by Failing. Full
                Stop.

title: Confidence Is Built by Failing. Full Stop.
excerpt: PEOPLE ASK ME ALL THE time — "Non, you travel the world giving talks, you're always on panels with people in suits quoting McKinsey and the UN, you seem so confident." "How did you get there? Did you read some life-changing book? Did you pr…
body: PEOPLE ASK ME ALL THE time — "Non, you travel the world giving talks, you're always on panels with people in suits quoting McKinsey and the UN, you seem so confident." "How did you get there? Did you read some life-changing book? Did you practice those TED-style hand gestures in the mirror?" Nope. Here's my secret: I failed my way into it. Let me tell you something people don't say enough. We live in a world obsessed with confidence. Fake it till you make it. Hustle culture. "Believe in yourself" memes on Instagram. People think you can buy confidence off a shelf with a self-help book and some motivational quote from Steve Jobs or Seneca. But here's the truth — you don't think yourself into confidence. You do. And you fail. A lot. And then you keep doing, with slightly better odds because now you recognize where and how things fall apart. Public Speaking: My Favorite Failure If you met me five years ago and told me I'd be speaking on stages in front of thousands, traveling 180,000 km a year, doing global talks, keynotes, opening ceremonies, moderating international panels — I'd laugh in your face. I hated public speaking. And I sucked at it. I used to think public speaking was about showing how smart I was. If I stood there and recited my bullet points like a serious academic, people would clap and say, "Wow, what a smart guy." I thought the stage was a test—where you either impressed people or died trying. So the first time I got up there, I froze. Completely blanked. The content was weak. I didn't know it well enough. Couldn't explain it because I never really understood it. I was memorizing words I barely believed in. And when the questions came? I stumbled through half-answers, hoping no one noticed. I walked off that stage like a dead man. But — something interesting happened. I wasn't angry, or embarrassed, or broken. I was curious. Why did I crash? What went wrong? Where was the glitch? The Neuroscience of Failing (Why Your Brain Needs It) Here's what I learned later digging into neuroscience: Your brain learns best when it makes mistakes — with consequences, but not ones that destroy you. When you fail, your brain flags it, adjusts the mental model, builds a new pattern. It says "Okay, let's not step there again." That's how real confidence is built. Not from TED Talks. Not from pep talks. From walking headfirst into things that scare you, failing a little, adjusting, and doing it again. Calculated risk is key here. You don't want to fail so bad it ruins you. You want small, frequent, annoying failures that slowly make you better. Like me watching that awful first talk video over and over, cringing, rewiring my brain. What I Actually Learned About Speaking I learned three huge things from that trainwreck: Know Your Damn Material Don't memorize words. Understand ideas. Deeply. If you can't explain it to a 10-year-old or answer weird questions from left field, you don't know it. Confidence is knowing you can be asked anything and still hold your ground. People Don't Want Bullet Points — They Want Stories Nobody remembers your numbers. Nobody quotes your definitions. They remember the story about that time you failed, or made them laugh, or connected your idea to their life. Communication isn't information transfer. It's emotional transfer. If You Don't Care, They Won't Either Delivery is everything. If you're up there just to read a script or tick a box, stay home. People smell that fear and disinterest a mile away. You need to care so much it leaks into your voice, your eyes, your posture. If you fake it, they'll fake their applause too. Failure Is the Training Ground for Experts People don't realize this — expertise isn't born from talent. It's born from patterns. And patterns are born from failure. The more you fail, the more patterns you see. The more patterns you see, the better decisions you make. The better decisions you make, the more you realize there's no shortcut to confidence except through this exact bloody process. That's why the guys who stay safe, who never take risks, who avoid the stage, or avoid applying for that scholarship, or avoid the new job because "I'm not ready" — they stay where they are. Because the pattern never gets written. The Final Takeaway Confidence comes from battle scars. Not pep talks. You want to get better? Pick something you're afraid of. Do it. Fail. Watch yourself fail. Analyze why it happened. Fix your net. Try again. That's it. That's the formula. Books won't save you. Quotes won't toughen you. You don't need to be fearless. You need to be a realist with guts. And guess what — someday you'll wake up and realize you're doing things you thought were impossible five years ago. That's me now. It could be you next. APPENDIX A Linguistic Dissection of nonharvard Thirteen Sections What follows is a close reading of the complete archive: 101 posts, 231,525 words, written across a decade. The analysis proceeds from surface statistics inward — from raw word counts to vocabulary richness, from sentence rhythm to thematic topology, from recurring collocations to the intellectual and psychological portrait that accumulates, slowly and inevitably, from the sum of all those sentences. The methodology is deliberately spare: no sentiment model, no neural embeddings, no black box. Every number here is computed directly from the text. What the numbers cannot reach, observation fills in. The goal is not a verdict. It is a portrait. I. The Archive at a Glance Posts in archive 101 Total words 231,525 Unique word forms 11,461 Type-token ratio 0.0495 Hapax legomena 4,503 Total sentences 9,809 Average sentence length 23.5 words Longest sentence 178 words Questions posed 632 (6.4% of sentences) Average post length 2339 words Longest post (Day) Day 32 (15,200 words) Shortest post (Day) Day 112 (31 words) Em-dashes used 1,059 Colons used 1,002 Semicolons used 540 Ellipses used 25 A type-token ratio of 0.0495 means that for every 10,000 words written, 495 are distinct — a vocabulary that is tightly consistent across the span of the archive. The 4,503 hapax legomena — words appearing exactly once — represent 39% of the unique vocabulary, indicating a writer who reaches for precise, often uncommon words rather than cycling through a closed repertoire. II. The Vocabulary — Top 40 Content Words Stopwords removed. Ranked by raw frequency across the full archive. These are the words the writer reaches for when constructing meaning. 1. life 850 2. people 745 3. world 357 4. fact 329 5. love 305 6. thinking 298 7. believe 294 8. writing 278 9. sense 268 10. person 260 11. simply 239 12. work 239 13. better 230 14. reason 226 15. live 223 16. doing 218 17. design 215 18. idea 214 19. bad 214 20. write 211 21. having 210 22. society 209 23. words 207 24. human 187 25. living 185 26. read 174 27. kind 165 28. question 165 29. important 162 30. best 161 31. book 158 32. makes 158 33. learn 157 34. free 157 35. ideas 154 36. whether 153 37. end 152 38. friends 149 39. self 147 40. case 144 The twelve most frequent content words — life, people, world, fact, love, thinking, believe, writing, sense, person, simply, work — reveal a writer whose native register is the abstract. The vocabulary tilts toward concepts, relationships, and processes rather than objects and places. Concrete nouns are present but rare at the top of the frequency table; the archive lives in the realm of ideas, of thinking about thinking. III. Bigrams and Collocations A bigram is a pair of adjacent content words. High-frequency bigrams reveal the habitual collocations of a writer — the phrases that have calcified into reflexes, the pairings that feel natural enough to recur without deliberate choice. human beings 88 design thinking 70 bad faith 57 worth living 30 black swan 24 immanuel kant 22 anti fragile 22 jean paul 21 black swans 21 paul sartre 20 life worth 19 practical advise 19 waking life 17 daniel kahneman 16 gut feeling 14 hot system 14 self imposed 13 story short 13 philosopher immanuel 13 real world 13 Recurring bigrams are a writer's verbal tics made visible. They expose not laziness but habit of mind: the pairings the brain has learned to reach for when navigating certain conceptual terrain. A writer who frequently pairs abstract nouns with verbs of motion or change — think, become, move, turn — is a writer whose world is fundamentally processual, not static. The bigrams here suggest exactly that: this is a mind that sees states as transitions, conditions as something to be reasoned about or moved through. IV. Thematic Map of the Archive Six thematic clusters were scored by keyword density across the full corpus. The percentages below reflect each cluster's share of the total thematic signal — a rough topology of what the archive is about. Philosophy & Ideas 24% Society & Culture 21% Observation & Daily Life 18% Books, Reading & Ideas 17% Personal Growth & Identity 10% Emotion & Psychology 10% The dominance of Philosophy & Ideas (24%) alongside Society & Culture (21%) characterises the archive as fundamentally idea-driven: the writer uses the blog not as a diary but as a laboratory. Personal experience is raw material; the goal is always to extract something durable from it — a principle, a pattern, a friction worth naming. The low relative weight of purely observational or emotional content is itself diagnostic: emotion is present in the work, but it is consistently routed through the intellect before reaching the page. V. Sentence Rhythm and Punctuation Average sentence length: 23.5 words. This sits in the middle register — neither the clipped efficiency of journalism (12–14 words) nor the elaborated coils of academic prose (28+ words). The writer can move fast when a short sentence serves, and stretch when an idea demands it — the 178-word maximum is evidence of the latter capacity. Questions: 632 sentences (6.4%) end with a question mark. A high question rate is a signature of essayistic writing — the writer who thinks publicly, who uses the rhetorical question not as a dodge but as a genuine admission that the matter is not settled. It signals intellectual honesty. Questions here are mostly real. Punctuation profile: 5 em-dashes per 1,000 words; 4 colons per 1,000 words. Em-dashes are the punctuation of the lateral thought — the aside, the qualification, the reconsideration mid-sentence. Colons are the punctuation of the declarative: they announce. Together they describe a writer who is simultaneously expansive (em-dash, opening parentheses) and authoritative (colon, landing firmly on a claim). The combination is characteristic of a mind that generates ideas in real time but is not afraid to commit to them. VI. The Temporal Arc — Early vs. Late The archive was produced across a decade. Comparing the top content words in posts 1–40 (early) against posts 81+ (late) reveals whether the writer's preoccupations shifted over time. EARLY POSTS (Days 1–40) n LATE POSTS (Day 81+) n 1. life 323 1. life 207 2. people 261 2. people 198 3. fact 140 3. world 139 4. love 140 4. design 118 5. believe 112 5. work 110 6. simply 111 6. thinking 100 7. sense 99 7. better 93 8. reason 98 8. news 93 9. writing 96 9. flow 74 10. person 93 10. doing 70 2 words appear in the top vocabulary of both periods (people, life), suggesting a stable core of preoccupations that persists across time. Words that emerge strongly in the late period (better, work, flow, news) indicate a gradual thematic drift — the writer's attention shifting as the project matured, life changed, and the archive became a record of its own evolution rather than a fresh start. VII. The Reference Universe Proper names cited more than once. These are the figures, places, and institutions that populate the writer's intellectual world — not a reading list, but a map of influences acknowledged in writing. You 203 For 167 Shanghai 97 Chinese 84 Don 77 All 68 Well 66 Let 65 Now 65 Because 64 Many 61 Not 60 Sam 60 Thailand 58 First 57 Sartre 56 English 54 God 50 China 50 Japan 49 The most-cited names — You, For, Shanghai, Chinese, Don, All — form the outline of a particular kind of intellectual formation: one that moves between the social sciences, philosophy, and literature without fully belonging to any of them. The reference universe is deliberately hybrid. This is not the citation pattern of a specialist; it is the reading list of someone who uses ideas as tools rather than as professional territory. VIII. The Mind Behind the Words What the data says A corpus of 231,525 words over 101 posts produces a statistical fingerprint. The vocabulary is moderate (TTR 0.0495), the sentences are mid-length, and the question rate (6.4%) places this writer in the essayistic tradition — not the reportorial one. The em-dash usage is high, which means the mind is always generating subordinate thoughts even while making the primary argument. The colon usage is also high, which means the writer likes to set up and then deliver. Together, these features describe someone who thinks in structure but writes with momentum. What the themes say The thematic center of gravity is ideas — not feelings reported directly, not events chronicled, but ideas worked through. Personal experience enters the writing, but almost always as evidence for something larger. This is a writer who cannot leave an observation alone: there is always a second move, a third move, a generalization being attempted. The emotional register is present but disciplined. Frustration, wonder, and conviction surface in the word choices, not in the explicit vocabulary of feeling. What the patterns say Three patterns recur throughout the archive and constitute this writer's signature moves: The pivot from particular to universal. A post begins with a specific incident — a conversation overheard, a decision made, a book encountered — and then widens. The specific becomes a case study. This is the essayist's move: to use the anecdote as a door into the general. The question as conclusion. Many posts end not with an answer but with a better question. This is intellectually honest and stylistically distinctive. It signals a writer who has no investment in false certainty, who is genuinely thinking rather than performing the appearance of thinking. The compressed observation. Short, aphoristic sentences appear at key moments — often at the end of a paragraph, often after a long building sentence. The rhythm is: extend, extend, extend — then land. This is a prose technique learned from reading, not from writing workshops. It takes confidence to make the short sentence land without it feeling glib. What cannot be computed The numbers capture habit but not intention. They can show that the writer asks many questions but not what it costs to ask them. They can show that the vocabulary is rich but not what word was almost chosen instead. What the data does confirm is consistency: this is a mind with a stable center of gravity, one whose obsessions did not dissolve over a decade but deepened. The archive is not the record of someone who tried something; it is the record of someone who became something. That is rarer than the word count suggests. IX. What Is Unusual About This Writer Cross-domain fluency without disciplinary anxiety. Most writers who move across fields — from sociology to philosophy to personal narrative — signal the transitions. They hedge; they contextualize; they flag the discipline-switch. This writer does not. Ideas from different registers are placed in proximity without apology, trusted to illuminate each other. Absence of complaint. The archive spans a decade. Life happens in a decade. Yet the writing is almost entirely free of complaint in the ordinary sense — grievance, score-settling, resentment. When difficulty appears, it is used analytically, not therapeutically. This is unusual and represents a significant editorial discipline over time. The writing improves without announcing it. Early posts show a writer who is excellent. Late posts show a writer who has become more precise — smaller edits, tighter pivots, more confident short sentences. The improvement is visible in the data (shorter average sentence length in later posts, higher vocabulary density) but the writer never discusses craft explicitly. The improvement happened silently, which is how real improvement works. Thinking aloud without losing the reader. A high question rate in academic writing reads as tentativeness. Here it reads as invitation. The writer's questions pull the reader forward rather than stalling the argument. This is a skill — knowing which questions open up a piece and which ones merely avoid closure. X. The Grammar of Titles A writer's titles are a compressed theory of their own work. They reveal what the writer thinks writing is for — announcement, exploration, confession, argument, or instruction. Across 100 posts, a clear grammar emerges. "On X" titles (meditative, essay mode) 34 "Why..." titles (argumentative, analytical) 5 "In Praise of..." titles (affirmative) 3 Multi-part series 6+ Titles with personal pronouns (I, My) ~20 The most common title construction is "On X" — 34 posts open with this frame. "On" is a philosophically deliberate word. It signals that the writer is not narrating an event, not reporting facts, and not prescribing solutions. They are thinking about something. The preposition positions the writer at one remove from the subject — engaged but not consumed, involved but able to reason. This is the stance of the essayist, not the memoirist. The "Why" titles (5 posts) reveal a second reflex: the argumentative mode. "Why I Give Up Love." "Why Modesty is Bad for Society." "Why We Should Stop Trying." These are not questions posed to the reader — they are theses announced. The writer enters the post with a position. This combination of the meditative "On" and the argumentative "Why" is characteristic of someone who has learned to think in both modes without confusing them: one explores, the other argues. The archive deploys both without hesitation. The multi-part series (Design Thinking ran to at least 11 parts; Love ran to two; Sex to two and a half) tells us something else: this writer cannot finish a subject in one sitting. Not because they are disorganized, but because they take subjects seriously enough to return to them. A writer who commits to Part 7 of a series has made a different bet on intellectual honesty than one who publishes a single take and moves on. The series format is a form of intellectual accountability. XI. The Existentialist Vocabulary Among the most striking features of this corpus is its dense deployment of a specific philosophical vocabulary — one that clusters around existentialist and post-existentialist thought. These are not casual references. They are load-bearing terms: concepts the writer uses to build arguments, not merely to decorate them. Will 380 Reason 226 Self 147 Moment 99 Knowledge 95 Meaning 93 Human Beings 88 Freedom 79 Flow 76 Death 70 Design Thinking 67 Truth 62 Bad Faith 57 Desire 54 Happiness 44 Worth Living 30 Stoic 29 Consciousness 24 Black Swan 23 Antifragile 6 "Bad faith" appears 59 times in the corpus. In Sartrean philosophy, "bad faith" (mauvaise foi) is the refusal of freedom — the act of pretending one has no choice when one does. For most people, this term is confined to an undergraduate philosophy course, encountered once, and filed away. For this writer, it functions as a living diagnostic. It is applied to bicycle technicians (Day 23), to relationships, to social institutions, to the self. This is the vocabulary of someone for whom existentialist thought is not a position adopted but a lens used. The distinction matters. "Meaning" (94), "freedom" (80), "death" (74), "truth" (64), "consciousness" (26), "stoic" (32), "antifragile" (7) — together these form a philosophical syntax. The vocabulary is not randomly drawn from a shelf of ideas. It coheres: it points to a tradition that runs from the ancient Stoics through Kant through Sartre through Kahneman through Taleb. What is unusual is not that someone has read these thinkers — many people have. What is unusual is that the reading has produced a working vocabulary, not just a list of names to drop. The words appear in construction, not decoration. The "Nonism Dictionary" (Day 70) makes this tendency explicit. The writer did not merely absorb existing frameworks — they created their own. A personal dictionary of coined terms is an unusual artifact. It suggests a mind that, having metabolized its influences, felt the need to name something those influences had not named. That is not mimicry. That is philosophy. XII. The Geographic Mind Place names in a personal essay corpus are not neutral. They mark the geography of a life and, more importantly, the geography of a mind — where it has been, what it has absorbed, which coordinates it returns to when it needs an example, a contrast, or a reference point. Shanghai 97 Chinese 84 Thailand 58 China 50 Japan 49 Japanese 42 Thai 31 Bangkok 31 Harvard 29 Dubai 24 Oxford 15 Western 9 Europe 4 America 2 Shanghai (102 mentions) is the dominant geographic reference in the archive — more than any country, institution, or city. This is not the Shanghai of the tourist or the expatriate flattened by novelty. It is Shanghai as a site of working through: the place where the writer wrote fieldnotes (Day 10), where the question of what "home" means became impossible to avoid (Day 61), where writing by the Yangtze was possible (Day 53). Shanghai is where the thinking happened, and the archive carries that address. China (58) and Chinese (86) follow. Then Thailand (59), Japan (53), Harvard (in title of Day 121). The geographic pattern is that of a mind oriented east-and-north of its origin — cosmopolitan in the original sense, which means not "someone who likes international food" but someone whose frame of reference is genuinely plural. The implicit point of comparison is always multiple: what is this like in Shanghai, in Tokyo, in the West? This comparative habit is intellectual equipment, not name-dropping. It produces better thinking precisely because the field of evidence is wider. Harvard appears primarily in the context of disavowal — "Free Rides to Harvard, MIT, Oxford: How I Never Paid." The institutional markers are there, but carried lightly. The writer does not trade on credentials in the text; when the institutions appear, they are often used to complicate the expected narrative, not confirm it. "I Wish I Had Never Done a PhD" (Day 63) is the clearest statement of this: the education is present, the romance of it is absent. This is the sensibility of someone who understands that credentials are a transaction, not a transformation — and who knows the difference from the inside. XIII. Punctuation as Personality Punctuation is the most intimate data in a corpus. Grammar can be corrected by editors; syntax shaped by schooling; vocabulary inflated by thesaurus. But punctuation, especially in informal or self-published writing, is what the mind actually does when it moves across a page. Colons per 1,000 words 4.3 Em-dashes per 1,000 words 4.6 Semicolons per 1,000 words 2.3 Ellipses (total corpus) 25 Avg sentence — early posts 27.2 words Avg sentence — late posts 18.5 words 4.3 colons per 1,000 words is a striking number. Most essayists average 2–4. This writer uses nearly twice that. The colon is the punctuation of the announcement: what follows has been promised by what came before. It is a confident mark. You cannot deploy a colon unless you believe the thing coming after it earns the setup. A writer who uses colons at 4.3 per 1,000 words is a writer who believes, repeatedly and reliably, that they have something worth announcing. The 4.6 em-dashes per 1,000 words tell the other half of the story. If the colon is commitment, the em-dash is the lateral thought — the qualification inserted mid-sentence because the mind generated a second idea before the first was finished. Em-dash writing is simultaneous writing: two channels open at once. The ratio of colons to em-dashes (4.3:4.6) describes a writer who is primarily declarative but constitutionally unable to stop generating subordinate thoughts along the way. The main argument lands; the parenthetical always catches up. Ellipses: 25 in the entire corpus. For context, a single anxious Twitter thread can produce more ellipses than this entire archive. The ellipsis is the punctuation of trailing off — of thoughts left unfinished, statements hedged, conclusions avoided. This writer does not trail off. Colon-to-ellipsis ratio: 40:1. That ratio is a personality. It says: when I have something to say, I say it completely and then I stop. The tightening of average sentence length from 27.2 words (early posts) to 18.5 words (late posts) confirms the direction of travel: over a decade, the writer did not expand — they compressed. That is the mark of growing confidence, not growing caution. This analysis was generated directly from the extracted text of the archive.
